subject: Physics
question_html: Explain the process of formation of 'depletion layer' and 'potential barrier' in a p-n junction region of a diode, with the help of a suitable diagram. Which feature of junction diode makes it suitable for its use as a rectifier?
question_text: Explain the process of formation of 'depletion layer' and 'potential barrier' in a p-n junction region of a diode, with the help of a suitable diagram. Which feature of junction diode makes it suitable for its use as a rectifier?
explanation: When an electron diffuses from n-side to p -side, it leaves behind an ionized donor on n side. Similarly when a hole diffuses from p-side to n-side, it leaves behind an ionized acceptor on {tex}p{/tex} side. This space charge region consisting of immobile ions on either side of the junction is known as depletion layer. As diffusion process continues, width of depletion layer increases and consequently strength of electric field increases across the junction and thus the drift current. The potential that prevents the movement of electron from {tex}n{/tex} region into p region is called potential barrier. Diode allows current to pass only when it is forward biased as resistance is small, whereas in reverse bias, its resistance is very large.
solution: When an electron diffuses from n-side to p -side, it leaves behind an ionized donor on n side. Similarly when a hole diffuses from p-side to n-side, it leaves behind an ionized acceptor on {tex}p{/tex} side. This space charge region consisting of immobile ions on either side of the junction is known as depletion layer. As diffusion process continues, width of depletion layer increases and consequently strength of electric field increases across the junction and thus the drift current. The potential that prevents the movement of electron from {tex}n{/tex} region into p region is called potential barrier. Diode allows current to pass only when it is forward biased as resistance is small, whereas in reverse bias, its resistance is very large.
solution_html:
When an electron diffuses from n-side to p -side, it leaves behind an ionized donor on n side.
Similarly when a hole diffuses from p-side to n-side, it leaves behind an ionized acceptor on {tex}p{/tex} side.
This space charge region consisting of immobile ions on either side of the junction is known as depletion layer.
As diffusion process continues, width of depletion layer increases and consequently strength of electric field increases across the junction and thus the drift current.
The potential that prevents the movement of electron from {tex}n{/tex} region into p region is called potential barrier.

Diode allows current to pass only when it is forward biased as resistance is small, whereas in reverse bias, its resistance is very large.

question_html: Differentiate between half-wave and full-wave rectification. With the help of a circuit diagram, explain the working of a full-wave rectifier.
question_text: Differentiate between half-wave and full-wave rectification. With the help of a circuit diagram, explain the working of a full-wave rectifier.
explanation: In half wave rectification there is output in one half of input cycle, whereas in full wave rectification, output is obtained for both half cycles of input (positive and negative) Working of full wave rectifier: Suppose the input voltage to A with respect to the centre-tap at any instant is positive. At that instant, voltage at B being out of phase will be negative. So, diode {tex}{D}_1{/tex} gets forward biased and conducts (while {tex}{D}_2{/tex} being reverse biased is not conducting). Hence, during this positive half cycle we get an output current (and output voltage across the load resistor {tex}{R}_{{L}}{/tex} ). In the course of ac cycle when the voltage at A becomes negative with respect to centre tap, the voltage at B would be positive. In this part of the cycle diode {tex}{D}_1{/tex} would not conduct but diode {tex}{D}_2{/tex} would, giving an output current and output voltage (across {tex}{R}_{{L}}{/tex} ) during the negative half cycle of the input ac.
solution: In half wave rectification there is output in one half of input cycle, whereas in full wave rectification, output is obtained for both half cycles of input (positive and negative) Working of full wave rectifier: Suppose the input voltage to A with respect to the centre-tap at any instant is positive. At that instant, voltage at B being out of phase will be negative. So, diode {tex}{D}_1{/tex} gets forward biased and conducts (while {tex}{D}_2{/tex} being reverse biased is not conducting). Hence, during this positive half cycle we get an output current (and output voltage across the load resistor {tex}{R}_{{L}}{/tex} ). In the course of ac cycle when the voltage at A becomes negative with respect to centre tap, the voltage at B would be positive. In this part of the cycle diode {tex}{D}_1{/tex} would not conduct but diode {tex}{D}_2{/tex} would, giving an output current and output voltage (across {tex}{R}_{{L}}{/tex} ) during the negative half cycle of the input ac.
solution_html:
In half wave rectification there is output in one half of input cycle, whereas in full wave rectification, output is obtained for both half cycles of input (positive and negative)
Working of full wave rectifier:

Suppose the input voltage to A with respect to the centre-tap at any instant is positive. At that instant, voltage at B being out of phase will be negative. So, diode {tex}{D}_1{/tex} gets forward biased and conducts (while {tex}{D}_2{/tex} being reverse biased is not conducting). Hence, during this positive half cycle we get an output current (and output voltage across the load resistor {tex}{R}_{{L}}{/tex}). In the course of ac cycle when the voltage at A becomes negative with respect to centre tap, the voltage at B would be positive. In this part of the cycle diode {tex}{D}_1{/tex} would not conduct but diode {tex}{D}_2{/tex} would, giving an output current and output voltage (across {tex}{R}_{{L}}{/tex}) during the negative half cycle of the input ac.

subject: Physics
question_html:
1. Draw the energy-band diagrams for conductors, semiconductors and insulators at {tex}{T}=0 {~K}{/tex}. How is an electron-hole pair formed in a semiconductor at room temperature?
  2. Carbon and silicon both, are members of IV group of periodic table and have the same lattice structure. Carbon is an insulator whereas silicon is a semiconductor. Explain.
question_text: Draw the energy-band diagrams for conductors, semiconductors and insulators at {tex}{T}=0 {~K}{/tex} . How is an electron-hole pair formed in a semiconductor at room temperature? Carbon and silicon both, are members of IV group of periodic table and have the same lattice structure. Carbon is an insulator whereas silicon is a semiconductor. Explain.
explanation: At room temperature, thermal energy is sufficient for electrons to make them free from the bonds and create a vacancy called hole. Hence electron hole pair is formed. The valence electron in carbon and silicon lie in the second and third orbit respectively. So, the energy required to take out an electron will be less for silicon as compared to carbon. Hence number of free electrons for conduction in silicon are significant but negligibly small for carbon.
solution: At room temperature, thermal energy is sufficient for electrons to make them free from the bonds and create a vacancy called hole. Hence electron hole pair is formed. The valence electron in carbon and silicon lie in the second and third orbit respectively. So, the energy required to take out an electron will be less for silicon as compared to carbon. Hence number of free electrons for conduction in silicon are significant but negligibly small for carbon.
solution_html:
1. At room temperature, thermal energy is sufficient for electrons to make them free from the bonds and create a vacancy called hole. Hence electron hole pair is formed.
  2. The valence electron in carbon and silicon lie in the second and third orbit respectively. So, the energy required to take out an electron will be less for silicon as compared to carbon. Hence number of free electrons for conduction in silicon are significant but negligibly small for carbon.

subject: Physics
question_html:
1. What are majority and minority charge carriers in an extrinsic semiconductor?
  2. A p-n junction is forward biased. Describe the movement of the charge carriers which produce current in it.
  3. The graph shows the variation of current with voltage for a p-n junction diode.

    Estimate the dynamic resistance of diode at {tex}{V}=-0.6{/tex} volt.
question_text: What are majority and minority charge carriers in an extrinsic semiconductor? A p-n junction is forward biased. Describe the movement of the charge carriers which produce current in it. The graph shows the variation of current with voltage for a p-n junction diode. Estimate the dynamic resistance of diode at {tex}{V}=-0.6{/tex} volt.
explanation: In an extrinsic semiconductor, the charge carriers whose number density is large are known as majority charge carriers. In an extrinsic semiconductor, the charge carriers whose number density is small are known as minority charge carriers. Due to the applied forward voltage, electrons from n-side cross the depletion region and reach p-side. Similarly, holes from p-side cross the junction and reach the n-side. Due to the movement of these charge carriers current is produced. At V = -0.6 volt, I = 0, so dynamic resistance is infinite.
solution: In an extrinsic semiconductor, the charge carriers whose number density is large are known as majority charge carriers. In an extrinsic semiconductor, the charge carriers whose number density is small are known as minority charge carriers. Due to the applied forward voltage, electrons from n-side cross the depletion region and reach p-side. Similarly, holes from p-side cross the junction and reach the n-side. Due to the movement of these charge carriers current is produced. At V = -0.6 volt, I = 0, so dynamic resistance is infinite.
solution_html:
1. In an extrinsic semiconductor, the charge carriers whose number density is large are known as majority charge carriers.
    In an extrinsic semiconductor, the charge carriers whose number density is small are known as minority charge carriers.
  2. Due to the applied forward voltage, electrons from n-side cross the depletion region and reach p-side. Similarly, holes from p-side cross the junction and reach the n-side. Due to the movement of these charge carriers current is produced.
  3. At V = -0.6 volt, I = 0, so dynamic resistance is infinite.

question_html: In an n-type semiconductor electron-hole combination is a continuous process at room temperature. Yet the electron concentration is always greater than the hole concentration in it. Explain.
question_text: In an n-type semiconductor electron-hole combination is a continuous process at room temperature. Yet the electron concentration is always greater than the hole concentration in it. Explain.
explanation: In a doped semiconductor the total number of conduction electrons is due to the electrons contributed by donors and those generated intrinsically, while the total number of holes is only due to the holes from the intrinsic sources.
solution: In a doped semiconductor the total number of conduction electrons is due to the electrons contributed by donors and those generated intrinsically, while the total number of holes is only due to the holes from the intrinsic sources.
solution_html: In a doped semiconductor the total number of conduction electrons is due to the electrons contributed by donors and those generated intrinsically, while the total number of holes is only due to the holes from the intrinsic sources.

subject: Physics
question_html:
1. What are majority and minority charge carriers of p-type and n-type semiconductors?
  2. Explain briefly the formation of diffusion current and drift current in a p-n junction diode.
question_text: What are majority and minority charge carriers of p-type and n-type semiconductors? Explain briefly the formation of diffusion current and drift current in a p-n junction diode.
explanation: In p-type semiconductor Majority charge carriers - holes Minority charge carriers - electrons In n-type semiconductors Majority charge carriers - electrons Minority charge carriers - holes Diffusion current - during the formation of p n junction, and due to the concentration gradient across {tex}p{/tex} and {tex}n-{/tex} sides, holes diffuse from {tex}p{/tex} side to {tex}n{/tex} side {tex}(p \rightarrow n){/tex} and electrons diffuse from {tex}n-{/tex} side to {tex}p-{/tex} side {tex}(n \rightarrow p){/tex} . This motion of charge carriers gives rise to diffusion current across the junction. Drift current - Due to electric field at junction, an electron on p - side of the junction moves to n - side and a hole on n - side of the junction moves to p side. This motion of charge carriers due to electric field gives drift current.
solution: In p-type semiconductor Majority charge carriers - holes Minority charge carriers - electrons In n-type semiconductors Majority charge carriers - electrons Minority charge carriers - holes Diffusion current - during the formation of p n junction, and due to the concentration gradient across {tex}p{/tex} and {tex}n-{/tex} sides, holes diffuse from {tex}p{/tex} side to {tex}n{/tex} side {tex}(p \rightarrow n){/tex} and electrons diffuse from {tex}n-{/tex} side to {tex}p-{/tex} side {tex}(n \rightarrow p){/tex} . This motion of charge carriers gives rise to diffusion current across the junction. Drift current - Due to electric field at junction, an electron on p - side of the junction moves to n - side and a hole on n - side of the junction moves to p side. This motion of charge carriers due to electric field gives drift current.
solution_html:
1. In p-type semiconductor
    Majority charge carriers - holes
    Minority charge carriers - electrons
    In n-type semiconductors
    Majority charge carriers - electrons
    Minority charge carriers - holes
  2. Diffusion current - during the formation of p n junction, and due to the concentration gradient across {tex}p{/tex} and {tex}n-{/tex} sides, holes diffuse from {tex}p{/tex} side to {tex}n{/tex} side {tex}(p \rightarrow n){/tex} and electrons diffuse from {tex}n-{/tex} side to {tex}p-{/tex} side {tex}(n \rightarrow p){/tex}. This motion of charge carriers gives rise to diffusion current across the junction.
    Drift current - Due to electric field at junction, an electron on p - side of the junction moves to n - side and a hole on n - side of the junction moves to p side. This motion of charge carriers due to electric field gives drift current.

subject: Physics
question_html:
Read the following paragraph and answer the questions that follow. (4 {tex}\times{/tex} 1 = 4)
Extrinsic semiconductors are made by doping pure or intrinsic semiconductors with suitable impurity. There are two type of dopants used in doping, Si or Ge, and using them p-type and n-type semiconductors can be obtained. A p-n junction is the basic building block of many semiconductor devices. Two important processes occur during the formation of a p-n junction: diffusion and drift. When such a junction is formed, a 'depletion layer' is created consisting of immobile ion-cores. This is responsible for a junction potential barrier. The width of a depletion layer and the height of potential barrier changes when a junction is forward-biased or reverse-biased. A semiconductor diode is basically a p-n junction with metallic contacts provided at the ends for application of an external voltage. Using diodes, alternating voltages can be rectified.

  1. Which of the following is a donor impurity atom for Ge?
    1. Boron
    2. Antimony
    3. Aluminium
    4. Indium
  2. When a pentavalent atom occupies the position of an atom in the crystal lattice of Si, four of its electrons form covalent bonds with four silicon neighbours, while the fifth remains bound to the parent atom. The energy required to set this electron free is about:
    1. 0.5 eV
    2. 0.1 eV
    3. 0.05 eV
    4. 0.01 eV
  3. During formation of a p-n junction:
    1. a layer of negative charge on {tex}n{/tex}-side and a layer of positive charge on p-side appear.
    2. a layer of positive charge on {tex}n{/tex}-side and a layer of negative charge on p-side appear.
    3. the electrons on p -side of the junction move to n -side initially.
    4. initially diffusion current is small and drift current is large.
    1. In reverse-biased p-n junction:
      1. the drift current is of the order of few mA.
      2. the applied voltage mostly drops across the depletion region.
      3. the depletion region width decreases.
      4. the current increases with increase in applied voltage.

        OR

    2. The output frequency of a full-wave rectifier with 50 Hz as input frequency is:
      1. 25 Hz
      2. 50 Hz
      3. 100 Hz
      4. 200 Hz
question_text: Read the following paragraph and answer the questions that follow. (4 {tex}\times{/tex} 1 = 4) Extrinsic semiconductors are made by doping pure or intrinsic semiconductors with suitable impurity. There are two type of dopants used in doping, Si or Ge, and using them p-type and n-type semiconductors can be obtained. A p-n junction is the basic building block of many semiconductor devices. Two important processes occur during the formation of a p-n junction: diffusion and drift. When such a junction is formed, a 'depletion layer' is created consisting of immobile ion-cores. This is responsible for a junction potential barrier. The width of a depletion layer and the height of potential barrier changes when a junction is forward-biased or reverse-biased. A semiconductor diode is basically a p-n junction with metallic contacts provided at the ends for application of an external voltage. Using diodes, alternating voltages can be rectified. Which of the following is a donor impurity atom for Ge? Boron Antimony Aluminium Indium When a pentavalent atom occupies the position of an atom in the crystal lattice of Si, four of its electrons form covalent bonds with four silicon neighbours, while the fifth remains bound to the parent atom. The energy required to set this electron free is about: 0.5 eV 0.1 eV 0.05 eV 0.01 eV During formation of a p-n junction: a layer of negative charge on {tex}n{/tex} -side and a layer of positive charge on p-side appear. a layer of positive charge on {tex}n{/tex} -side and a layer of negative charge on p-side appear. the electrons on p -side of the junction move to n -side initially. initially diffusion current is small and drift current is large. In reverse-biased p-n junction: the drift current is of the order of few mA. the applied voltage mostly drops across the depletion region. the depletion region width decreases. the current increases with increase in applied voltage. OR The output frequency of a full-wave rectifier with 50 Hz as input frequency is: 25 Hz 50 Hz 100 Hz 200 Hz
explanation: (B) Antimony (C) 0.05 eV (B) A layer of positive charge on n side and a layer of negative charge on p side appear (B) The applied voltage mostly drops across the depletion region OR (C) 100 Hz
solution: (B) Antimony (C) 0.05 eV (B) A layer of positive charge on n side and a layer of negative charge on p side appear (B) The applied voltage mostly drops across the depletion region OR (C) 100 Hz
solution_html:
1. (B) Antimony
  2. (C) 0.05 eV
  3. (B) A layer of positive charge on n side and a layer of negative charge on p side appear
    1. (B) The applied voltage mostly drops across the depletion region

      OR

question_html:
Read the following paragraph and answer the questions that follow. (4 {tex}\times{/tex} 1 = 4)
According to the band theory of solids, a semiconductor has a valence band and a conduction band separated by a gap, known as energy band gap. Pure semiconductors are called intrinsic semiconductors. At room temperature, some electrons from the valence band can acquire enough energy to cross the band gap and enter the conduction band. The number of conduction electrons is equal to the number of holes is an intrinsic semiconductor. The number of charge carriers can be changed by doping of a suitable impurity in a pure semiconductor. Such semiconductors are known as extrinsic semiconductors. These are of two types (n-type and p-type).
A p-n junction is the basic building block of semiconductor devices. Two important processes occur during formation of a p-n junction: diffusion and drift. A 'depletion layer' is formed in a p-n junction. This is responsible for a junction potential barrier. A semiconductor diode is basically a p-n junction with metallic contacts provided at the ends for the application of an external voltage. A diode can be forward-biased or reverse-biased. The barrier height and the depletion layer width in a p-n junction changes depending on the nature of the biasing.

    1. Which of the following statements is not true? (1)
      1. The resistance of an intrinsic semiconductor decreases with the increase of temperature.
      2. Doping pure Si with trivalent impurities gives p-type semiconductors.
      3. The majority charge carriers in n-type semiconductors are holes.
      4. A p-n junction can act as a semiconductor diode.

        OR

    2. In a unbiased p-n junction: (1)
      1. the diffusion current is zero everywhere.
      2. the drift current is zero everywhere.
      3. the electric potential is zero everywhere.
      4. the drift current and the diffusion current cancel each other.
  2. The impurity atoms with which pure Ge should be doped to convert it into an n-type semiconductor, is: (1)
    1. Boron
    2. Phosphorous
    3. Aluminium
    4. Indium
  3. The energy band gap in Ge at 0 K is about: (1)
    1. 0.72 eV
    2. {tex}1 \cdot 1 {eV}{/tex}
    3. 3.0 eV
    4. 5.4 eV
  4. In a p-n junction diode under reverse bias, the barrier height: (1)
    1. is reduced and the depletion layer width decreases.
    2. is reduced and the depletion layer width increases.
    3. increases and the depletion layer width also increases.
    4. increases and the depletion layer width decreases.

subject: Physics
question_html: How does the energy gap of an intrinsic semiconductor effectively change when doped with a (a) trivalent impurity, and (b) pentavalent impurity? Justify your answer in each case.
question_text: How does the energy gap of an intrinsic semiconductor effectively change when doped with a (a) trivalent impurity, and (b) pentavalent impurity? Justify your answer in each case.
explanation: Doping with a trivalent impurity (e.g., aluminum) introduces "holes" as charge carriers, creating a p-type semiconductor . The energy gap remains the same, but the effective energy levels shift, making it easier for electrons from the valence band to move to the holes, enhancing conductivity. Doping with a pentavalent impurity (e.g., phosphorus) adds extra electrons, resulting in an n-type semiconductor . Again, the energy gap stays unchanged, but the conduction band has additional energy levels close to it, allowing more electrons to contribute to conductivity.
solution: Doping with a trivalent impurity (e.g., aluminum) introduces "holes" as charge carriers, creating a p-type semiconductor . The energy gap remains the same, but the effective energy levels shift, making it easier for electrons from the valence band to move to the holes, enhancing conductivity. Doping with a pentavalent impurity (e.g., phosphorus) adds extra electrons, resulting in an n-type semiconductor . Again, the energy gap stays unchanged, but the conduction band has additional energy levels close to it, allowing more electrons to contribute to conductivity.
solution_html:
1. Doping with a trivalent impurity (e.g., aluminum) introduces "holes" as charge carriers, creating a p-type semiconductor. The energy gap remains the same, but the effective energy levels shift, making it easier for electrons from the valence band to move to the holes, enhancing conductivity.

  2. Doping with a pentavalent impurity (e.g., phosphorus) adds extra electrons, resulting in an n-type semiconductor. Again, the energy gap stays unchanged, but the conduction band has additional energy levels close to it, allowing more electrons to contribute to conductivity.

subject: Physics
question_html: Differentiate between diffusion current and drift current. Explain their role in the formation of p-n junction.
question_text: Differentiate between diffusion current and drift current . Explain their role in the formation of p-n junction.
explanation: Diffusion current: It is due to the flow of majority charge carriers. Drift current: It is due to the flow of minority charge carriers. Due to the concentration gradient across p-, and n- sides, holes diffuse from p- side to n- side & electrons diffuse from n- side to p- side. This motion of charge carriers gives rise to diffusion current across the junction. Due to the positive space- charge region on n-side and negative space - charge region on p-side, an electric field directed from positive charge towards negative charge develops. Due to this field, motion of charge carriers comes into play giving rise to drift current.
solution: Diffusion current: It is due to the flow of majority charge carriers. Drift current: It is due to the flow of minority charge carriers. Due to the concentration gradient across p-, and n- sides, holes diffuse from p- side to n- side & electrons diffuse from n- side to p- side. This motion of charge carriers gives rise to diffusion current across the junction. Due to the positive space- charge region on n-side and negative space - charge region on p-side, an electric field directed from positive charge towards negative charge develops. Due to this field, motion of charge carriers comes into play giving rise to drift current.
solution_html:
Diffusion current: It is due to the flow of majority charge carriers.
Drift current: It is due to the flow of minority charge carriers.
Due to the concentration gradient across p-, and n- sides, holes diffuse from p- side to n- side & electrons diffuse from n- side to p- side. This motion of charge carriers gives rise to diffusion current across the junction. Due to the positive space- charge region on n-side and negative space - charge region on p-side, an electric field directed from positive charge towards negative charge develops. Due to this field, motion of charge carriers comes into play giving rise to drift current.

question_html:
Junction Diode as a Rectifier:
The process of conversion of an ac voltage into a dc voltage is called rectification and the device which performs this conversion is called a rectifier. The characteristics of a p-n junction diode reveal that when a p-n junction diode is forward biased, it offers a low resistance and when it is reverse biased, it offers a high resistance. Hence, a p-n junction diode conducts only when it is forward biased. This property of a p-n junction diode makes it suitable for its use as a rectifier.
Thus, when an ac voltage is applied across a p-n junction, it conducts only during those alternate half cycles for which it is forward biased. A rectifier which rectifies only half cycle of an ac voltage is called a half-wave rectifier and one that rectifies both the half cycles is known as a full-wave rectifier.


The root mean square value of an alternating voltage applied to a full-wave rectifier is {tex}\frac{{V}_0}{\sqrt{2}}{/tex}. Then the root mean square value of the rectified output voltage is:

question_html:
Explain the following, giving reasons:

  1. A doped semiconductor is electrically neutral.
  2. In a {tex}{p}-{n}{/tex} junction under equilibrium, there is no net current.
  3. In a diode, the reverse current is practically not dependent on the applied voltage.
question_text: Explain the following, giving reasons: A doped semiconductor is electrically neutral. In a {tex}{p}-{n}{/tex} junction under equilibrium, there is no net current. In a diode, the reverse current is practically not dependent on the applied voltage.
explanation: Charge of additional charge carriers is just equal and opposite to that of the ionised cores in the lattice. Under equilibrium, the diffusion current is equal to the drift current. Reverse current is limited due to concentration of minority charge carriers on either side of the junction.
solution: Charge of additional charge carriers is just equal and opposite to that of the ionised cores in the lattice. Under equilibrium, the diffusion current is equal to the drift current. Reverse current is limited due to concentration of minority charge carriers on either side of the junction.
solution_html:
1. Charge of additional charge carriers is just equal and opposite to that of the ionised cores in the lattice.
  2. Under equilibrium, the diffusion current is equal to the drift current.
  3. Reverse current is limited due to concentration of minority charge carriers on either side of the junction.

1. Explain the characteristics of a {tex}{p}-{n}{/tex} junction diode that makes it suitable for its use as a rectifier.
  2. With the help of a circuit diagram, explain the working of a full wave rectifier.
question_text: Explain the characteristics of a {tex}{p}-{n}{/tex} junction diode that makes it suitable for its use as a rectifier. With the help of a circuit diagram, explain the working of a full wave rectifier.
explanation: p-n junction diode allows current to pass only when it is forward biased When input voltage to A, with respect to the centre tap at any instant is positive, at that instant voltage at B, being out of phase will be negative, diode {tex}D_{1}{/tex} gets forward biased and conducts while {tex}D_{2}{/tex} being reverse biased does not conduct. Hence during this half cycle an output current and output voltage across {tex}{R}_{{L}}{/tex} is obtained. During second half of the cycle when voltage at A becomes negative with respect to centre tap, the voltage at B would be positive. Hence {tex}{D}_{1}{/tex} would not conduct but {tex}{D}_{2}{/tex} would be giving an output current and output voltage. Thus output voltage is obtained during both halves of the cycle.
solution: p-n junction diode allows current to pass only when it is forward biased When input voltage to A, with respect to the centre tap at any instant is positive, at that instant voltage at B, being out of phase will be negative, diode {tex}D_{1}{/tex} gets forward biased and conducts while {tex}D_{2}{/tex} being reverse biased does not conduct. Hence during this half cycle an output current and output voltage across {tex}{R}_{{L}}{/tex} is obtained. During second half of the cycle when voltage at A becomes negative with respect to centre tap, the voltage at B would be positive. Hence {tex}{D}_{1}{/tex} would not conduct but {tex}{D}_{2}{/tex} would be giving an output current and output voltage. Thus output voltage is obtained during both halves of the cycle.
solution_html:
1. p-n junction diode allows current to pass only when it is forward biased
  2.  
    When input voltage to A, with respect to the centre tap at any instant is positive, at that instant voltage at B, being out of phase will be negative, diode {tex}D_{1}{/tex} gets forward biased and conducts while {tex}D_{2}{/tex} being reverse biased does not conduct. Hence during this half cycle an output current and output voltage across {tex}{R}_{{L}}{/tex} is obtained. During second half of the cycle when voltage at A becomes negative with respect to centre tap, the voltage at B would be positive. Hence {tex}{D}_{1}{/tex} would not conduct but {tex}{D}_{2}{/tex} would be giving an output current and output voltage. Thus output voltage is obtained during both halves of the cycle.

subject: Physics
question_html: Explain the terms depletion layer and potential barrier for a junction diode and their formation.
question_text: Explain the terms depletion layer and potential barrier for a junction diode and their formation.
explanation: During the formation of {tex}{p}-{n}{/tex} junction due to the concentration gradient holes diffuse from p-side to n-side leaving behind an immobile ionized acceptor (negative charge), hence forming a layer of negative charge (negative space-charge region) on p-side. Similarly, a layer of positive charge is developed on the n-side of the junction (positive space-charge region). This space - charge region on either side of the junction together is known as depletion layer. Due to the positive space- charge region on the n-side of the junction & negative space - charge region on the p-side of the junction a potential difference is developed across the junction of the two regions, which is called potential barrier.
solution: During the formation of {tex}{p}-{n}{/tex} junction due to the concentration gradient holes diffuse from p-side to n-side leaving behind an immobile ionized acceptor (negative charge), hence forming a layer of negative charge (negative space-charge region) on p-side. Similarly, a layer of positive charge is developed on the n-side of the junction (positive space-charge region). This space - charge region on either side of the junction together is known as depletion layer. Due to the positive space- charge region on the n-side of the junction & negative space - charge region on the p-side of the junction a potential difference is developed across the junction of the two regions, which is called potential barrier.
solution_html:
During the formation of {tex}{p}-{n}{/tex} junction due to the concentration gradient holes diffuse from p-side to n-side leaving behind an immobile ionized acceptor (negative charge), hence forming a layer of negative charge (negative space-charge region) on p-side.
Similarly, a layer of positive charge is developed on the n-side of the junction (positive space-charge region). This space - charge region on either side of the junction together is known as depletion layer.
Due to the positive space- charge region on the n-side of the junction & negative space - charge region on the p-side of the junction a potential difference is developed across the junction of the two regions, which is called potential barrier.

subject: Physics
question_html:
A pure semiconductor like Ge or Si, when doped with a small amount of suitable impurity, becomes an extrinsic semiconductor. In thermal equilibrium, the electron and hole concentration in it are related to the concentration of intrinsic charge carriers. A p -type or n -type semiconductor can be converted into a {tex}{p}-{n}{/tex} junction by doping it with suitable impurity. Two processes, diffusion and drift take place during formation of a {tex}p-n{/tex} junction. A semiconductor diode is basically a {tex}p-n{/tex} junction with metallic contacts provided at the ends for the application of an external voltage. A p-n junction diode allows currents to pass only in one direction when it is forward biased. Due to this property, a diode is widely used to rectify alternating voltages, in half-wave or full wave configuration. ({tex}4 \times 1=4{/tex})

  1. When Ge is doped with pentavalent impurity, the energy required to free the weakly bound electron from the dopant is about
    (A) 0.001 eV
    (B) 0.01 eV
    (C) 0.72 eV
    (D) 1.1 eV
  2. At a given temperature, the number of intrinsic charge carriers in a semiconductor is {tex}2.0 \times 10^{10} {~cm}^{-3}{/tex}. It is doped with pentavalent impurity atoms. As a result, the number of holes in it becomes {tex}8 \times 10^{3} {~cm}^{-3}{/tex}. The number of electrons in the semiconductor is
    (A) {tex}2 \times 10^{24} {~m}^{-3}{/tex}
    (B) {tex}4 \times 10^{23} {~m}^{-3}{/tex}
    (C) {tex}1 \times 10^{22} {~m}^{-3}{/tex}
    (D) {tex}5 \times 10^{22} {~m}^{-3}{/tex}
    1. During the formation of a {tex}{p}-{n}{/tex} junction -
      (A) electrons diffuse from p-region into n-region and holes diffuse from n -region into p-region.
      (B) both electrons and holes diffuse from n -region into p -region.
      (C) electrons diffuse from n -region into p -region and holes diffuse from p-region into n-region.
      (D) both electrons and holes diffuse from p-region into n-region.

      OR

    2. Initially during the formation of a {tex}{p}-{n}{/tex} junction -
      (A) diffusion current is large and drift current is small.
      (B) diffusion current is small and drift current is large.
      (C) both the diffusion and the drift currents are large.
      (D) both the diffusion and the drift currents are small.
  4. An ac voltage {tex}{V}=0.5 \sin (100 \pi {t}){/tex} volt is applied, in turn, across a half-wave rectifier and a full-wave rectifier. The frequency of the output voltage across them respectively will be
    (A) {tex}25 {~Hz}, 50 {~Hz}{/tex}
    (B) {tex}25 {~Hz}, 100 {~Hz}{/tex}
    (C) {tex}50 {~Hz}, 50 {~Hz}{/tex}
    (D) {tex}50 {~Hz}, 100 {~Hz}{/tex}

question_html:
The process of converting ac into dc is called rectification and the device used is called a rectifier. When ac signal is fed to a junction diode during positive half cycle, the diode is forward biased and current flows through it. During the negative half cycle, the diode is reverse biased and it does not conduct. Thus the ac signal is rectified. The p-n junction diodes can be used as half-wave and full-wave rectifiers. (4 {tex}\times{/tex} 1 = 4)

  1. Which bulb/bulbs will glow in the given circuit?

    (A) {tex}{B}_{1}{/tex} only
    (B) {tex}{B}_{2}{/tex} only
    (C) Both {tex}{B}_{1}{/tex} and {tex}{B}_{2}{/tex}
    (D) Neither {tex}{B}_{1}{/tex} nor {tex}{B}_{2}{/tex}
    1. A full-wave rectifier circuit is shown in the figure. The contribution in output waveform from junction diode {tex}{D}_{1}{/tex} is:

      (A) A, D
      (B) A, C
      (C) {tex}{B}, {D}{/tex}
      (D) B, C

      OR

    2. The output in a half-wave rectifier is:
      (A) unidirectional without ripple
      (B) steady and continuous
      (C) unidirectional with ripple
      (D) steady but discontinuous
  3. In a p-n junction diode, the majority charge carriers on p-side and on n-side are, respectively :
    (A) electrons, electrons
    (B) electrons, holes
    (C) holes, holes
    (D) holes, electrons
  4. If the frequency of the half-wave rectifier is 50 Hz, the frequency of full-wave rectifier is:
    (A) 25 Hz
    (B) 50 Hz
    (C) 100 Hz
    (D) 200 Hz

subject: Physics
question_html: Briefly explain how the diffusion and drift currents contribute to the formation of potential barrier in a p-n junction diode.
question_text: Briefly explain how the diffusion and drift currents contribute to the formation of potential barrier in a p-n junction diode.
explanation: The diffusion current due to concentration gradient at the junction forms a space charge region consisting of immobile charge carriers. Due to this an electric field is generated at the junction giving rise to drift current in a direction opposite to diffusion current. The potential at which diffusion current becomes equal to drift current is called potential barrier.
solution: The diffusion current due to concentration gradient at the junction forms a space charge region consisting of immobile charge carriers. Due to this an electric field is generated at the junction giving rise to drift current in a direction opposite to diffusion current. The potential at which diffusion current becomes equal to drift current is called potential barrier.
solution_html:
The diffusion current due to concentration gradient at the junction forms a space charge region consisting of immobile charge carriers. Due to this an electric field is generated at the junction giving rise to drift current in a direction opposite to diffusion current.
The potential at which diffusion current becomes equal to drift current is called potential barrier.

  2. Impurities are added in intrinsic semiconductors.
question_text: Answer the following giving reasons: A p-n junction diode is damaged by a strong current. Impurities are added in intrinsic semiconductors.
explanation: Due to strong current, a junction diode gets heated, consequently large number of covalent bonds are broken and the junction is damaged. Deliberate addition of impurity atoms in intrinsic semiconductor increases its conductivity and is suitable for making electronic devices.
solution: Due to strong current, a junction diode gets heated, consequently large number of covalent bonds are broken and the junction is damaged. Deliberate addition of impurity atoms in intrinsic semiconductor increases its conductivity and is suitable for making electronic devices.
solution_html:

  2. Deliberate addition of impurity atoms in intrinsic semiconductor increases its conductivity and is suitable for making electronic devices.

question_html:
1. A germanium crystal is doped with antimony. With the help of energy-band diagram, explain how the conductivity of the doped crystal is affected.
  2. Briefly explain the two processes involved in the formation of a p-n junction.
  3. What will the effect of (1) forward biasing, and (2) reverse biasing be on the width of depletion layer in a p-n junction diode?
question_text: A germanium crystal is doped with antimony. With the help of energy-band diagram, explain how the conductivity of the doped crystal is affected. Briefly explain the two processes involved in the formation of a p-n junction. What will the effect of (1) forward biasing, and (2) reverse biasing be on the width of depletion layer in a p-n junction diode?
explanation: With proper level of doping, the number of conduction electrons can be made much larger than the number of holes. Due to this conductivity of the doped crystal increases. Two processes (a) Diffusion (b) drift Diffusion: Due to concentration gradient majority charge carrier that is electron moves from n {tex}\to{/tex} p side and holes to p {tex}\to{/tex} n side. This movement of charges is called diffusion. Drift: Due to the junction field, an electron on p-side of the junction moves to n- side and a hole on n- side of the junction moves to p- side. The motion of the charge carrier due to electric field is called drift. (1) The width of depletion layer in a p-n junction diode decreases in forward biasing. (2) The width of depletion layer in a p-n junction diode decreases in reverse biasing increases.
solution: With proper level of doping, the number of conduction electrons can be made much larger than the number of holes. Due to this conductivity of the doped crystal increases. Two processes (a) Diffusion (b) drift Diffusion: Due to concentration gradient majority charge carrier that is electron moves from n {tex}\to{/tex} p side and holes to p {tex}\to{/tex} n side. This movement of charges is called diffusion. Drift: Due to the junction field, an electron on p-side of the junction moves to n- side and a hole on n- side of the junction moves to p- side. The motion of the charge carrier due to electric field is called drift. (1) The width of depletion layer in a p-n junction diode decreases in forward biasing. (2) The width of depletion layer in a p-n junction diode decreases in reverse biasing increases.
solution_html:
1. With proper level of doping, the number of conduction electrons can be made much larger than the number of holes. Due to this conductivity of the doped crystal increases.
     
  2. Two processes (a) Diffusion (b) drift
    Diffusion: Due to concentration gradient majority charge carrier that is electron moves from n {tex}\to{/tex} p side and holes to p {tex}\to{/tex} n side. This movement of charges is called diffusion.
    Drift: Due to the junction field, an electron on p-side of the junction moves to n- side and a hole on n- side of the junction moves to p- side. The motion of the charge carrier due to electric field is called drift.
  3. (1) The width of depletion layer in a p-n junction diode decreases in forward biasing.
    (2) The width of depletion layer in a p-n junction diode decreases in  reverse biasing increases.

  2. In a silicon diode, the current increases from 10 mA to 20 mA when the voltage changes from 0.6 V to 0.7 V. Calculate the dynamic resistance of the diode.
question_text: Briefly describe the classification of solids into metals, insulators and semi-conductors on the basis of energy level diagrams. In a silicon diode, the current increases from 10 mA to 20 mA when the voltage changes from 0.6 V to 0.7 V. Calculate the dynamic resistance of the diode.
explanation: For Eg > 3eV material is insulate For Eg < 3eV material is semiconductor For Eg = 0 or overlapping of conduction and valence band material is conductor. {tex}\mathrm{r}_{\mathrm{d}}=\frac{\Delta V}{\Delta I}=\frac{0.7-0.6}{(20-10) \times 10^{-3}}{/tex} r d = 10 {tex}\Omega{/tex} Hence, the dynamic resistance of the diode = 10 {tex}\Omega{/tex} .
solution: For Eg > 3eV material is insulate For Eg < 3eV material is semiconductor For Eg = 0 or overlapping of conduction and valence band material is conductor. {tex}\mathrm{r}_{\mathrm{d}}=\frac{\Delta V}{\Delta I}=\frac{0.7-0.6}{(20-10) \times 10^{-3}}{/tex} r d = 10 {tex}\Omega{/tex} Hence, the dynamic resistance of the diode = 10 {tex}\Omega{/tex} .
solution_html:
1. For Eg > 3eV material is insulate
    For Eg < 3eV material is semiconductor
    For Eg = 0 or overlapping of conduction and valence band material is conductor.
  2. {tex}\mathrm{r}_{\mathrm{d}}=\frac{\Delta V}{\Delta I}=\frac{0.7-0.6}{(20-10) \times 10^{-3}}{/tex}
    rd = 10 {tex}\Omega{/tex}
    Hence, the dynamic resistance of the diode = 10 {tex}\Omega{/tex}.

question_text: With the help of a circuit diagram, briefly explain the working of a full-wave rectifier using p-n junction diodes. Draw V - I characteristics of a p-n junction diode. Explain how these characteristics make a diode suitable for rectification. Carbon and silicon have the same lattice structure. Then why is carbon an insulator but silicon a semiconductor?
explanation: Working: Suppose the input voltage to A with respect to the centre tap at any instant is positive. At that instant voltage at B, being out of phase will be negative. So diode D 1 gets forward biased and conducts, while D 2 being reverse biased has very high resistance so it does not conduct. Similarly during second half of the cycle polarity get reversed so only D 2 will conduct. So the ac voltage gets rectified. This diagram shows that the diode conducts when forward biased and does not conduct when reverse biased. This characteristics makes it suitable for use for rectification. The 4 bonding of electrons of C and Si lie respectively, in the second and third orbit. Hence energy required to take out an electron from their atoms will be much less than that for C. Hence number of free e - for conduction in Si significant but negligibly small for C. So carbon resistivity is very high and it can not be placed in the category of semiconductors.

Hence number of free e- for conduction in Si significant but negligibly small for C. So carbon resistivity is very high and it can not be placed in the category of semiconductors.

subject: Physics
question_html: Explain the formation of depletion layer and barrier potential in a p-n junction diode.
question_text: Explain the formation of depletion layer and barrier potential in a p-n junction diode.
explanation: At the junction there is diffusion of charge carriers due to thermal agitation; so that some of electrons of n-region diffuse to p-region while some of holes of p-region diffuse into n-region. Some charge carriers combine with opposite charges to neutralise each other. Thus near the junction there is an excess of positively charged ions in n-region and an excess of negatively charged ions in p-region. This sets up a potential difference called potential barrier and hence an internal electric field E i across the junctions. The field E i is directed from n-region to p-region. This field stops the further diffusion of charge carriers. Thus the layers (≈10 -4 cm to 10 -6 cm) on either side of the junction becomes free from mobile charge carriers and hence is called the depletion layer. The symbol of p-n junction diode is shown in Fig.
solution: At the junction there is diffusion of charge carriers due to thermal agitation; so that some of electrons of n-region diffuse to p-region while some of holes of p-region diffuse into n-region. question_html:
Draw V-I characteristics of a p-n junction diode. Answer the following giving reasons:

  1. Why is the reverse bias current almost independent of applied voltage up to breakdown voltage?
  2. Why does the reverse current show a sudden increase at breakdown voltage?
question_text: Draw V-I characteristics of a p-n junction diode. Answer the following giving reasons: Why is the reverse bias current almost independent of applied voltage up to breakdown voltage? Why does the reverse current show a sudden increase at breakdown voltage?
explanation: For V - l characteristic of a p-n junction diode, see figure. A very small current of order of few {tex}\mu{/tex} A flows, when the diode is reverse biased. It is due to the drift of minority charge carriers whose number density remains constant, so the current under reverse bias is almost independent of the applied potential upto a critical voltage. When the reverse voltage across the p-n junction reaches a critical voltage, the reverse current abruptly increases to a large value. It is due to the large increase in the number of minority charge carriers because of the breakdown of the diode. The avalanche breakdown occurs in lightly doped diodes due to ionisation by collision. Zener breakdown occurs at low voltages in heavily doped diodes by field emission.
solution: For V - l characteristic of a p-n junction diode, see figure. A very small current of order of few {tex}\mu{/tex} A flows, when the diode is reverse biased. It is due to the drift of minority charge carriers whose number density remains constant, so the current under reverse bias is almost independent of the applied potential upto a critical voltage. When the reverse voltage across the p-n junction reaches a critical voltage, the reverse current abruptly increases to a large value. It is due to the large increase in the number of minority charge carriers because of the breakdown of the diode. The avalanche breakdown occurs in lightly doped diodes due to ionisation by collision. Zener breakdown occurs at low voltages in heavily doped diodes by field emission.

For V - l characteristic of a p-n junction diode, see figure.
 

  1. A very small current of order of few {tex}\mu{/tex}A flows, when the diode is reverse biased. It is due to the drift of minority charge carriers whose number density remains constant, so the current under reverse bias is almost independent of the applied potential upto a critical voltage.
  2. When the reverse voltage across the p-n junction reaches a critical voltage, the reverse current abruptly increases to a large value. It is due to the large increase in the number of minority charge carriers because of the breakdown of the diode. The avalanche breakdown occurs in lightly doped diodes due to ionisation by collision. Zener breakdown occurs at low voltages in heavily doped diodes by field emission.

question_html: Define barrier potential. Why does the thickness of the depletion layer in a p-n junction diode vary with increase in reverse bias?
question_text: Define barrier potential. Why does the thickness of the depletion layer in a p-n junction diode vary with increase in reverse bias?
explanation: Barrier potential is the potential difference required by the electrons in the PN junction diode to pass the electric field across the semiconductors. During reverse biased conditions the p junction of the diode is connected to the negative of the battery and N junction to the positive of the battery. Thus the holes of the P side are pulled away from junction and electrons form N side leave the charged ions and thus the thickness of the depletion layer increases.
solution: Barrier potential is the potential difference required by the electrons in the PN junction diode to pass the electric field across the semiconductors. During reverse biased conditions the p junction of the diode is connected to the negative of the battery and N junction to the positive of the battery. Thus the holes of the P side are pulled away from junction and electrons form N side leave the charged ions and thus the thickness of the depletion layer increases.
solution_html:
Barrier potential is the potential difference required by the electrons in the PN junction diode to pass the electric field across the semiconductors.
During reverse biased conditions the p junction of the diode is connected to the negative of the battery and N junction to the positive of the battery. Thus the holes of the P side are pulled away from junction and electrons form N side leave the charged ions and thus the thickness of the depletion layer increases.

question_html: Give two differences between a half-wave rectifier and a full-wave rectifier.
question_text: Give two differences between a half-wave rectifier and a full-wave rectifier.
explanation: Full wave rectifier rectifies the negative component of voltage and converts it into DC whereas half wave rectifier rectifies the negative component of voltage only. Efficiency of the full wave rectifier is greater than the half-wave rectifier.
solution: Full wave rectifier rectifies the negative component of voltage and converts it into DC whereas half wave rectifier rectifies the negative component of voltage only. Efficiency of the full wave rectifier is greater than the half-wave rectifier.

  2. Efficiency of the full wave rectifier is greater than the half-wave rectifier.

explanation: C 1 is n-type semiconductor and C 2 is the p-type semiconductor. Doping of intrinsic semiconductors is required to increase the majority of charge carriers based on which n-type and p-type semiconductors can be made and thus can be used.

  2. Doping of intrinsic semiconductors is required to increase the majority of charge carriers based on which n-type and p-type semiconductors can be made and thus can be used.

subject: Physics
question_html:
1. Explain the formation of a p-n junction.
  2. Can we take one slab of p-type semiconductor and physically join it to another n-type semiconductor to get a p-n junction? Explain.
question_text: Explain the formation of a p-n junction. Can we take one slab of p-type semiconductor and physically join it to another n-type semiconductor to get a p-n junction? Explain.
explanation: During the formation of p-n junction due to concentration gradient across p and n sides, holes diffuse from p side to n side and electrons diffuse from n side to p side This motion of charge carriers gives rise to diffusion current across the junction. Diffusion of electrons develops a layer of positive charge on n side of the junction and diffusion of holes develops a layer of negative charge on p side of the junction. Due to this space charge region on either side of the junction an electric field is developed. This electric field drifts charge carriers across the junction and sets up drift current in a direction opposite to diffusion current. This process continues until the diffusion current is equal to drift current. Thus p-n junction is formed. No, Any slab, howsoever flat, will have roughness much larger than the inter-atomic spacing ( {tex}\sim{/tex} 2 to 3 {tex}\overset \circ A{/tex} ) and hence continuous contact at the atomic level will not be possible. The junction will behave as a discontinuity for the flowing charge carriers.
solution: During the formation of p-n junction due to concentration gradient across p and n sides, holes diffuse from p side to n side and electrons diffuse from n side to p side This motion of charge carriers gives rise to diffusion current across the junction. Diffusion of electrons develops a layer of positive charge on n side of the junction and diffusion of holes develops a layer of negative charge on p side of the junction. Due to this space charge region on either side of the junction an electric field is developed. This electric field drifts charge carriers across the junction and sets up drift current in a direction opposite to diffusion current. This process continues until the diffusion current is equal to drift current. Thus p-n junction is formed. No, Any slab, howsoever flat, will have roughness much larger than the inter-atomic spacing ( {tex}\sim{/tex} 2 to 3 {tex}\overset \circ A{/tex} ) and hence continuous contact at the atomic level will not be possible. The junction will behave as a discontinuity for the flowing charge carriers.
solution_html:
During the formation of p-n junction due to concentration gradient across p and n sides, holes diffuse from p side to n side and electrons diffuse from n side to p side This motion of charge carriers gives rise to diffusion current across the junction.
Diffusion of electrons develops a layer of positive charge on n side of the junction and diffusion of holes develops a layer of negative charge on p side of the junction.
Due to this space charge region on either side of the junction an electric field is developed. This electric field drifts charge carriers across the junction and sets up drift current in a direction opposite to diffusion current.
This process continues until the diffusion current is equal to drift current. Thus p-n junction is formed.
No, Any slab, howsoever flat, will have roughness much larger than the inter-atomic spacing ({tex}\sim{/tex}2 to 3 {tex}\overset \circ A{/tex}) and hence continuous contact at the atomic level will not be possible. The junction will behave as a discontinuity for the flowing charge carriers.

    1. The waves from two light sources should have zero or constant phase difference.

subject: Physics
question_html:
Explain how the barrier potential is formed in a p-n junction. How is it affected in

  1. forward bias, and
  2. reverse bias?
question_text: Explain how the barrier potential is formed in a p-n junction. How is it affected in forward bias, and reverse bias?
explanation: In a p-n-junction diode, the loss of electrons from the n-region and the gain of electrons by the p-region causes a difference of potential across the junction of the two regions. This potential tends to prevent the movement of electrons from the n region to the p region. Hence a potential barrier is created across the junction. In forward bias, the barrier potential is reduced. In reserve bias. the barrier potential increases.
solution: In a p-n-junction diode, the loss of electrons from the n-region and the gain of electrons by the p-region causes a difference of potential across the junction of the two regions. This potential tends to prevent the movement of electrons from the n region to the p region. Hence a potential barrier is created across the junction. In forward bias, the barrier potential is reduced. In reserve bias. the barrier potential increases.
solution_html:
In a p-n-junction diode, the loss of electrons from the n-region and the gain of electrons by the p-region causes a difference of potential across the junction of the two regions. This potential tends to prevent the movement of electrons from the n region to the p region. Hence a potential barrier is created across the junction.

  1. In forward bias, the barrier potential is reduced.
  2. In reserve bias. the barrier potential increases.

  2. Doping of intrinsic semiconductors is a necessity for making electronic devices.
  3. Photodiodes are operated in reverse bias.
question_text: The resistance of a p-n junction is low when it is forward biased and is high when it is reversed biased. Doping of intrinsic semiconductors is a necessity for making electronic devices. Photodiodes are operated in reverse bias.
explanation: When a p-n junction is forward biased, the junction width decreases and as a result, its resistance also decreases. On the other hand, when a p-n junction is reverse biased, the junction width increases hence resistance increases. Conductivity of intrinsic semi-conductors is very low. Hence, no electronic device can be developed using them. Dopping increases conductivity, hence makes intrinsic semiconductor suitable for making electronic devices. It is easier to observe the change in the current with change in light intensity if a reverse bias is applied.
solution: When a p-n junction is forward biased, the junction width decreases and as a result, its resistance also decreases. On the other hand, when a p-n junction is reverse biased, the junction width increases hence resistance increases. Conductivity of intrinsic semi-conductors is very low. Hence, no electronic device can be developed using them. Dopping increases conductivity, hence makes intrinsic semiconductor suitable for making electronic devices. It is easier to observe the change in the current with change in light intensity if a reverse bias is applied.
solution_html:
1. When a p-n junction is forward biased, the junction width decreases and as a result, its resistance also decreases.
    On the other hand, when a p-n junction is reverse biased, the junction width increases hence resistance increases.
  2. Conductivity of intrinsic semi-conductors is very low. Hence, no electronic device can be developed using them.
    Dopping increases conductivity, hence makes intrinsic semiconductor suitable for making electronic devices.
  3. It is easier to observe the change in the current with change in light intensity if a reverse bias is applied.

question_html:
Explain the terms depletion layer and potential barrier in a p-n junction diode. How are the

  1. width of depletion layer, and
  2. value of potential barrier affected when the p-n junction is forward biased?
question_text: Explain the terms depletion layer and potential barrier in a p-n junction diode. How are the width of depletion layer, and value of potential barrier affected when the p-n junction is forward biased?
explanation: Potential barrier: The accumulation of negative charges in the p-region and positive charges in the n-region sets up a potential difference across the junction. This acts as a barrier and is called barrier potential. Depletion layer: It is a layer of the immobile ion formed near the p-n junction by diffusion of majority charge carriers and electron-hole recombination. Width of the depletion layer decreases in forward bias. The value of the potential barrier reduces in forward bias.
solution: Potential barrier: The accumulation of negative charges in the p-region and positive charges in the n-region sets up a potential difference across the junction. This acts as a barrier and is called barrier potential. Depletion layer: It is a layer of the immobile ion formed near the p-n junction by diffusion of majority charge carriers and electron-hole recombination. Width of the depletion layer decreases in forward bias. The value of the potential barrier reduces in forward bias.
solution_html:
Potential barrier: The accumulation of negative charges in the p-region and positive charges in the n-region sets up a potential difference across the junction. This acts as a barrier and is called barrier potential.
Depletion layer: It is a layer of the immobile ion formed near the p-n junction by diffusion of majority charge carriers and electron-hole recombination.

  1. Width of the depletion layer decreases in forward bias.
  2. The value of the potential barrier reduces in forward bias.

question_html: Can we take one slab of p-type semiconductor and physically join it to another n-type semiconductor to get p-n junction?
question_text: Can we take one slab of p-type semiconductor and physically join it to another n-type semiconductor to get p-n junction?
explanation: No. Any slab, howsoever flat, will have roughness much larger than the inter-atomic crystal spacing (~2 to 3 {tex}\overset{o} A {/tex} ) and hence continuous contact at the atomic level will not be possible. The junction will behave as a discontinuity for the flowing charge carriers.
solution: No. Any slab, howsoever flat, will have roughness much larger than the inter-atomic crystal spacing (~2 to 3 {tex}\overset{o} A {/tex} ) and hence continuous contact at the atomic level will not be possible. The junction will behave as a discontinuity for the flowing charge carriers.

subject: Physics
question_html: Explain the formation of potential barrier and depletion region in a p-n junction diode. What is effect of applying forward bias on the width of depletion region?
question_text: Explain the formation of potential barrier and depletion region in a p-n junction diode. What is effect of applying forward bias on the width of depletion region?
explanation: Formation of depletion region: In the p-type semiconductor, holes are the majority carrier and in the n-type semiconductor, electrons are the majority carrier. When a p-n junction is formed, some of the electrons from the n-region which have reached the conduction band are free to diffuse across the junction and combine with holes. Filling a hole, makes a negative ion in p-side and a positive ion in the n-side. Thus, free charges get depleted and a depletion region is formed, which inhibits any further electron transfer. Applying forward bias, the depletion region reduces and again electrons can diffuse.
solution: Formation of depletion region: In the p-type semiconductor, holes are the majority carrier and in the n-type semiconductor, electrons are the majority carrier. When a p-n junction is formed, some of the electrons from the n-region which have reached the conduction band are free to diffuse across the junction and combine with holes. Filling a hole, makes a negative ion in p-side and a positive ion in the n-side. Thus, free charges get depleted and a depletion region is formed, which inhibits any further electron transfer. Applying forward bias, the depletion region reduces and again electrons can diffuse.
solution_html:
Formation of depletion region: In the p-type semiconductor, holes are the majority carrier and in the n-type semiconductor, electrons are the majority carrier.
When a p-n junction is formed, some of the electrons from the n-region which have reached the conduction band are free to diffuse across the junction and combine with holes.
Filling a hole, makes a negative ion in p-side and a positive ion in the n-side. Thus, free charges get depleted and a depletion region is formed, which inhibits any further electron transfer.

Applying forward bias, the depletion region reduces and again electrons can diffuse.

question_html: In a p-n junction diode, the forward bias resistance is low as compared to the reverse bias resistance. Give reason.
question_text: In a p-n junction diode, the forward bias resistance is low as compared to the reverse bias resistance. Give reason.
explanation: In a forward biased p-n junction, potential barrier is lowered and hence the electrons and holes can easily cross the junction. In reversed biased p-n junction, the potential barrier is raised and hence the electrons and holes cannot easily cross the junction. For this reason, forward bias resistance is low compared to reverse biased resistance of p-n junction.
solution: In a forward biased p-n junction, potential barrier is lowered and hence the electrons and holes can easily cross the junction. In reversed biased p-n junction, the potential barrier is raised and hence the electrons and holes cannot easily cross the junction. For this reason, forward bias resistance is low compared to reverse biased resistance of p-n junction.

In reversed biased p-n junction, the potential barrier is raised and hence the electrons and holes cannot easily cross the junction. For this reason, forward bias resistance is low compared to reverse biased resistance of p-n junction.

explanation: Diffusion current is set up across the junction due to the concentration difference of the majority charge carriers on the two sides of the junction. This diffusion develops an electric field from n- side to p- side across the junction which creates a drift current in the opposite direction.
solution: Diffusion current is set up across the junction due to the concentration difference of the majority charge carriers on the two sides of the junction. This diffusion develops an electric field from n- side to p- side across the junction which creates a drift current in the opposite direction.
solution_html:
Diffusion current is set up across the junction due to the concentration difference of the majority charge carriers on the two sides of the junction.
This diffusion develops an electric field from n- side to p- side across the junction which creates a drift current in the opposite direction.

subject: Physics
question_html:
1. Why is an intrinsic semiconductor deliberately converted into an extrinsic semiconductor by adding impurity atoms?
  2. Explain briefly the two processes that occur in p-n junction region to create a potential barrier.
question_text: Why is an intrinsic semiconductor deliberately converted into an extrinsic semiconductor by adding impurity atoms? Explain briefly the two processes that occur in p-n junction region to create a potential barrier.
explanation: To increase the electrical conductivity / to increase the number density of charge carriers some impurities are deliberately added in the pure semiconductors. Diffusion and Drift Explanation Diffusion: During the formation of p-n junction, due to the concentration gradient across the p and n sides, the motion of majority charge carriers give rise to diffusion current. Drift: Due to the electric field developed at the junction, the motion of the minority charge carriers due to the force of electric field is called drift. With the passage of time, diffusion current decreases whereas drift current increases and balance each other. This, creates a potential barrier.
solution: To increase the electrical conductivity / to increase the number density of charge carriers some impurities are deliberately added in the pure semiconductors. Diffusion and Drift Explanation Diffusion: During the formation of p-n junction, due to the concentration gradient across the p and n sides, the motion of majority charge carriers give rise to diffusion current. Drift: Due to the electric field developed at the junction, the motion of the minority charge carriers due to the force of electric field is called drift. With the passage of time, diffusion current decreases whereas drift current increases and balance each other. This, creates a potential barrier.
solution_html:
1. To increase the electrical conductivity / to increase the number density of charge carriers some impurities are deliberately added in the pure semiconductors.
  2. Diffusion and Drift
    Explanation
    Diffusion: During the formation of p-n junction, due to the concentration gradient across the p and n sides, the motion of majority charge carriers give rise to diffusion current.
    Drift: Due to the electric field developed at the junction, the motion of the minority charge carriers due to the force of electric field is called drift.
    With the passage of time, diffusion current decreases whereas drift current increases and balance each other. This, creates a potential barrier.

question_text: How are energy bands formed in a crystalline solid? Draw the energy band diagrams for p-type and n-type semiconductors. Depict the donor/acceptor energy levels in these diagrams and write their significance.
explanation: Isolated atoms have discrete energy levels. In a crystalline solid, due to the presence of large number of atoms, interatomic interactions take place between atoms. Due to which, energy levels get modified to energy bands and we get band energy levels. Significance : The number of electrons/ holes per unit volume increases in the donor/acceptor energy levels.
solution: Isolated atoms have discrete energy levels. In a crystalline solid, due to the presence of large number of atoms, interatomic interactions take place between atoms. 1. Isolated atoms have discrete energy levels. In a crystalline solid, due to the presence of large number of atoms, interatomic interactions take place between atoms. Due to which, energy levels get modified to energy bands and we get band energy levels.
  2.                                                                               Significance : The number of electrons/ holes per unit volume increases in the donor/acceptor energy levels.

explanation: An enormously large number of energy levels closely spaced in a very small energy range constitute an energy band. The highest energy band occupied by the valence electrons is called the valence band and the next empty allowed band is called the conduction band. Metal Semiconductor

question_html: Draw the circuit diagram of a full wave rectifier and explain its working. Also, give the input and output waveforms.
question_text: Draw the circuit diagram of a full wave rectifier and explain its working. Also, give the input and output waveforms.
explanation: Full wave Rectifier Working:- The AC input voltage across secondary s 1 and s 2 changes polarity after each half cycle. Suppose during the first half cycle of input AC signal, the terminal s 1 is positive relative to centre tap O and s 2 is negative relative to O. then diode D 1 is forward bias and diode D 2 is reverse biased. Therefore diode D 1 conduct while diode D 2 does not. In the next half cycle, the terminal s 1 is negative relative to centre tap O and s 2 is positive relative to O. then diode D 2 is forward bias and diode D 1 is reverse biased. Therefore diode D 2 conduct while diode D 1 does not. The direction of current in resistance RL is the same from A to B for both half cycles. Hence for input AC signal, the output current is a continuous series of unidirectional pulses. full-wave rectifier and output and input waveforms are shown in the figure.

In the case of n-type Si-semiconductor, the donor energy level is slightly below the bottom of conduction band whereas in p-type semiconductor, the acceptor energy level is slightly above the top of valence band. Explain, giving examples, what role do these energy levels play in conduction and valence bands.
question_text: Draw the energy band diagram of (i) n-type, and (ii) p-type semiconductors at temperature T > 0 K. In the case of n-type Si-semiconductor, the donor energy level is slightly below the bottom of conduction band whereas in p-type semiconductor, the acceptor energy level is slightly above the top of valence band. Explain, giving examples, what role do these energy levels play in conduction and valence bands.
explanation: Energy band diagrams of n-type and p-type semiconductors are shown below: In the case of n-type semiconductors, The Donor energy level decreases the energy gap between the conduction band and the valence band, electrons from donor impurity atoms will move into the conduction band with a very small supply of energy. Hence, the conduction band will, therefore, have electrons as majority charge carriers. In the case of p-type semiconductor, a very small supply of energy can cause an electron from its valence band to jump to the acceptor energy level. Hence, the valence band will have a dominant density of holes which shows that holes are the majority charge carriers in p-type semiconductors.

subject: Physics
question_html: With what considerations in view is a photodiode fabricated? Explain its working with the help of a suitable diagram. With the help of V-I characteristics, state how photodiode is used to detect optical signals.
question_text: With what considerations in view is a photodiode fabricated? Explain its working with the help of a suitable diagram. With the help of V-I characteristics, state how photodiode is used to detect optical signals.
explanation: A photodiode is fabricated with a transparent window to allow light to fall on the diode. and the generation of e-h pairs takes place in or near the depletion region of the diode. Working of photodiode - When photodiode is illuminated with light of suitable frequency, the electron hole pairs are generated near depletion region due to its specific fabrication. The junction potential separates electrons and holes before their recombination and e - releases to n - side and holes reaches to p - side to the direction of electric field and hence current flows across the photodiode when connected with load. If reverse bias is applied across the photodiode, the photo current changes with the change in intensity of light. Hence, it can be used to detect the optical signals.
solution: A photodiode is fabricated with a transparent window to allow light to fall on the diode. and the generation of e-h pairs takes place in or near the depletion region of the diode. Working of photodiode - When photodiode is illuminated with light of suitable frequency, the electron hole pairs are generated near depletion region due to its specific fabrication. The junction potential separates electrons and holes before their recombination and e - releases to n - side and holes reaches to p - side to the direction of electric field and hence current flows across the photodiode when connected with load. If reverse bias is applied across the photodiode, the photo current changes with the change in intensity of light. Hence, it can be used to detect the optical signals.
solution_html:
A photodiode is fabricated with a transparent window to allow light to fall on the diode. and the generation of e-h pairs takes place in or near the depletion region of the diode.

Working of photodiode -
When photodiode is illuminated with light of suitable frequency, the electron hole pairs are generated near depletion region due to its specific fabrication. The junction potential separates electrons and holes before their recombination and e- releases to n- side and holes reaches to p- side to the direction of electric field and hence current flows across the photodiode when connected with load.

If reverse bias is applied across the photodiode, the photo current changes with the change in intensity of light. Hence, it can be used to detect the optical signals.

subject: Physics
question_html: Can a transistor be used as a rectifier?
question_text: Can a transistor be used as a rectifier?
explanation: A transistor may be used as a rectifier. If we connect the base-emitter or base-collector region and use it as a diode, a transistor will work as an occasional current rectifier. It's just that standard rectifiers are cheaper and might rectify higher values of current yet, and hence, are preferred for rectification.
solution: A transistor may be used as a rectifier. If we connect the base-emitter or base-collector region and use it as a diode, a transistor will work as an occasional current rectifier. It's just that standard rectifiers are cheaper and might rectify higher values of current yet, and hence, are preferred for rectification.
solution_html: A transistor may be used as a rectifier. If we connect the base-emitter or base-collector region and use it as a diode, a transistor will work as an occasional current rectifier. It's just that standard rectifiers are cheaper and might rectify higher values of current yet, and hence, are preferred for rectification.
