Show that the voltage gain, A V , of the amplifier is given by {tex}A_{V}=-\frac{\beta_{a c} R_{L}}{r_{i}}{/tex} where {tex}\beta_{a c}{/tex} is the current gain, R L is the load resistance and r i is the input resistance of the transistor. What is the significance of the negative sign in the expression for the voltage gain?
explanation: Circuit diagram of CE transistor amplifier, When an ac input signal V l (to be amplified) is superimposed on the bias V BB , the output, which is measured between collector and ground, increases. We first assume that V i = 0 Applying Kirchoff's law to the output loop, V CC = V CE + I C R L Similarly, the input loop gives, V BB = V BE + I B R B When V i is not zero, we have V BE + V i = V BE + l B R B + {tex}\Delta{/tex} I B (R B + R i ) {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} V i = {tex}\Delta I_{B}\left(R_{B}+R_{i}\right){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}V_{i}=r \Delta I_{B}{/tex} Change in I B causes changes in I C Hence, {tex}\beta_{\mathrm{ac}}=\frac{\Delta I_{\mathrm{c}}}{\Delta l_{\mathrm{B}}}=\frac{\mathrm{I}_{\mathrm{C}}}{\mathrm{I}_{\mathrm{B}}}{/tex} As, {tex}\Delta V_{C C}=\Delta V_{C E}+R_{L} \Delta I_{C}{/tex} = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\Delta V_{C E}=-R_{L} \Delta I_{C}{/tex} The change in V CE is the output voltage V o is, {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}V_{0}=-R_{L} \Delta I_{C}{/tex} = {tex}\beta_{\mathrm{ac}} \Delta l_{\mathrm{B}} \mathrm{R}_{\mathrm{L}}{/tex} The voltage gain of the amplifier is, {tex}\mathrm{A}_{\mathrm{V}}=\frac{\mathrm{V}_{0}}{\mathrm{~V}_{\mathrm{i}}}=\frac{\Delta \mathrm{V}_{\mathrm{CE}}}{\mathrm{r} \Delta \mathrm{I}_{\mathrm{B}}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{-\beta_{\mathrm{ac}} \Delta l_{\mathrm{B}} R_{\mathrm{L}}}{\mathrm{r} \Delta \mathrm{I}_{\mathrm{B}}}=-\beta_{\mathrm{ac}} \frac{\mathrm{R}_{\mathrm{L}}}{\mathrm{r}}{/tex} The negative sign in the expression shows the output voltage and input voltage has a phase difference of {tex}\pi{/tex} .
solution: Circuit diagram of CE transistor amplifier, When an ac input signal V l (to be amplified) is superimposed on the bias V BB , the output, which is measured between collector and ground, increases. We first assume that V i = 0 Applying Kirchoff's law to the output loop, V CC = V CE + I C R L Similarly, the input loop gives, V BB = V BE + I B R B When V i is not zero, we have V BE + V i = V BE + l B R B + {tex}\Delta{/tex} I B (R B + R i ) {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} V i = {tex}\Delta I_{B}\left(R_{B}+R_{i}\right){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}V_{i}=r \Delta I_{B}{/tex} Change in I B causes changes in I C Hence, {tex}\beta_{\mathrm{ac}}=\frac{\Delta I_{\mathrm{c}}}{\Delta l_{\mathrm{B}}}=\frac{\mathrm{I}_{\mathrm{C}}}{\mathrm{I}_{\mathrm{B}}}{/tex} As, {tex}\Delta V_{C C}=\Delta V_{C E}+R_{L} \Delta I_{C}{/tex} = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\Delta V_{C E}=-R_{L} \Delta I_{C}{/tex} The change in V CE is the output voltage V o is, {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}V_{0}=-R_{L} \Delta I_{C}{/tex} = {tex}\beta_{\mathrm{ac}} \Delta l_{\mathrm{B}} \mathrm{R}_{\mathrm{L}}{/tex} The voltage gain of the amplifier is, {tex}\mathrm{A}_{\mathrm{V}}=\frac{\mathrm{V}_{0}}{\mathrm{~V}_{\mathrm{i}}}=\frac{\Delta \mathrm{V}_{\mathrm{CE}}}{\mathrm{r} \Delta \mathrm{I}_{\mathrm{B}}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{-\beta_{\mathrm{ac}} \Delta l_{\mathrm{B}} R_{\mathrm{L}}}{\mathrm{r} \Delta \mathrm{I}_{\mathrm{B}}}=-\beta_{\mathrm{ac}} \frac{\mathrm{R}_{\mathrm{L}}}{\mathrm{r}}{/tex} The negative sign in the expression shows the output voltage and input voltage has a phase difference of {tex}\pi{/tex} .
solution_html:
Circuit diagram of CE transistor amplifier,

When an ac input signal Vl (to be amplified) is superimposed on the bias VBB, the output, which is measured between collector and ground, increases.
We first assume that Vi = 0
Applying Kirchoff's law to the output loop,
VCC = VCE + ICRL 
Similarly, the input loop gives,
VBB = VBE + IBRB 
When Vi is not zero, we have
VBE + Vi = VBE + lBRB + {tex}\Delta{/tex} IB(RB + Ri) 
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} Vi = {tex}\Delta I_{B}\left(R_{B}+R_{i}\right){/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}V_{i}=r \Delta I_{B}{/tex} 
Change in IB causes changes in IC 
Hence, {tex}\beta_{\mathrm{ac}}=\frac{\Delta I_{\mathrm{c}}}{\Delta l_{\mathrm{B}}}=\frac{\mathrm{I}_{\mathrm{C}}}{\mathrm{I}_{\mathrm{B}}}{/tex} 
As, {tex}\Delta V_{C C}=\Delta V_{C E}+R_{L} \Delta I_{C}{/tex} = 0
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\Delta V_{C E}=-R_{L} \Delta I_{C}{/tex} 
The change in VCE is the output voltage Vo is,
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}V_{0}=-R_{L} \Delta I_{C}{/tex} 
= {tex}\beta_{\mathrm{ac}} \Delta l_{\mathrm{B}} \mathrm{R}_{\mathrm{L}}{/tex} 
The voltage gain of the amplifier is,
{tex}\mathrm{A}_{\mathrm{V}}=\frac{\mathrm{V}_{0}}{\mathrm{~V}_{\mathrm{i}}}=\frac{\Delta \mathrm{V}_{\mathrm{CE}}}{\mathrm{r} \Delta \mathrm{I}_{\mathrm{B}}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{-\beta_{\mathrm{ac}} \Delta l_{\mathrm{B}} R_{\mathrm{L}}}{\mathrm{r} \Delta \mathrm{I}_{\mathrm{B}}}=-\beta_{\mathrm{ac}} \frac{\mathrm{R}_{\mathrm{L}}}{\mathrm{r}}{/tex} 
The negative sign in the expression shows the output voltage and input voltage has a phase difference of  {tex}\pi{/tex}.

question_html: Explain, how a depletion region is formed in a junction diode?
question_text: Explain, how a depletion region is formed in a junction diode?
explanation: When a p-type crystal is placed in contact with n-type crystal so as to form one piece, the assembly so obtained is called p-n junction or junction diode or crystal diode. The surface of contact of p and n-type crystals is called junction. In the p-section, holes are the majority carriers; while in n-section, the majority carriers are electrons. Due to the high concentration of different types of charge carriers in the two sections, holes from p-region diffuse into n-region and electrons from n-region diffuse into p-region. In both cases, when an electron meets a hole, the two cancel the effect of each other and as a result, a thin layer at the junction becomes devoid of charge carriers. This is called the depletion layer or depletion region as shown in Fig.

Draw the circuit diagram showing how a p-n junction diode is

  1. forward biased
  2. reverse biased

How is the width of depletion layer affected in the two cases?
question_text: Draw the circuit diagram showing how a p-n junction diode is forward biased reverse biased How is the width of depletion layer affected in the two cases?
explanation: The forward-bias connections of a p-n junction are as shown in Fig. When the p-n junction is forward biased, the depletion layer becomes thin. It is because, the polarity of the external d.c. source opposes the fictitious battery developed across the junction. As a result, the potential drop across the junction decreases making the depletion layer thin. It leads to the low resistance of the junction diode during forward bias. The reverse-bias connections of a p-n junction are as shown in Fig. When the p-n junction is reverse biased, the depletion layer becomes thick. It is because, the external d.c. source aids the fictitious battery. It results in the increase of potential drop across the junction and the depletion layer appears thick. Because of the increased thickness of the depletion layer, the p-n junction offers high resistance during reverse bias.

subject: Physics
question_html: Name the important processes that occurs during the formation of a p-n junction. Explain briefly, with the help of a suitable diagram, how a p-n junction is formed. Define the term 'barrier potential'?
question_text: Name the important processes that occurs during the formation of a p-n junction. Explain briefly, with the help of a suitable diagram, how a p-n junction is formed. Define the term 'barrier potential'?
explanation: Two important processes occur during the formation of a p-n junction are diffusion and drift . In the p-section, holes are the majority carriers; while in n-section, the majority carriers are electrons. Due to the high concentration of different types of charge carriers in the two sections, holes from p-region diffuse into n-region and electrons from n-region diffuse into p-region. In both cases, when an electron meets a hole, the two cancel the effect of each other and as a result, a thin layer at the junction becomes devoid of charge carriers. This is called depletion layer as shown in Fig. Due to the positive space-charge region on n-side of the junction and negative space charge region on p-side of the junction, an electric field directed from positive charge towards negative charge develops. Due to this field, an electron on p-side of the junction moves to n-side and a hole on nside of the junction moves to p-side. The motion of charge carriers due to the electric field is called drift. Thus a drift current, which is opposite in direction to the diffusion current (Fig.) starts. Initially, diffusion current is large and drift current is small. As the diffusion process continues, the space-charge regions on either side of the junction extend, thus increasing the electric field strength and hence drift current. This process continues until the diffusion current equals the drift current. Thus a p-n junction is formed. When p-type and n-type semiconductors are joined together, because of diffusion of charge carriers through the junction, a barrier is formed which opposes the further diffusion of charges through the junction. This barrier is called potential barrier . Thus, potential barrier in a PN junction refers to the potential required to overcome the barrier at the PN junction.
solution: Two important processes occur during the formation of a p-n junction are diffusion and drift . In the p-section, holes are the majority carriers; while in n-section, the majority carriers are electrons. Due to the high concentration of different types of charge carriers in the two sections, holes from p-region diffuse into n-region and electrons from n-region diffuse into p-region. In both cases, when an electron meets a hole, the two cancel the effect of each other and as a result, a thin layer at the junction becomes devoid of charge carriers. This is called depletion layer as shown in Fig. Due to the positive space-charge region on n-side of the junction and negative space charge region on p-side of the junction, an electric field directed from positive charge towards negative charge develops. Due to this field, an electron on p-side of the junction moves to n-side and a hole on nside of the junction moves to p-side. The motion of charge carriers due to the electric field is called drift. Thus a drift current, which is opposite in direction to the diffusion current (Fig.) starts. Initially, diffusion current is large and drift current is small. As the diffusion process continues, the space-charge regions on either side of the junction extend, thus increasing the electric field strength and hence drift current. This process continues until the diffusion current equals the drift current. Thus a p-n junction is formed. When p-type and n-type semiconductors are joined together, because of diffusion of charge carriers through the junction, a barrier is formed which opposes the further diffusion of charges through the junction. This barrier is called potential barrier . Thus, potential barrier in a PN junction refers to the potential required to overcome the barrier at the PN junction.
solution_html:
Two important processes occur during the formation of a p-n junction are diffusion and drift.
In the p-section, holes are the majority carriers; while in n-section, the majority carriers are electrons. Due to the high concentration of different types of charge carriers in the two sections, holes from p-region diffuse into n-region and electrons from n-region diffuse into p-region. In both cases, when an electron meets a hole, the two cancel the effect of each other and as a result, a thin layer at the junction becomes devoid of charge carriers. This is called depletion layer as shown in Fig.

Due to the positive space-charge region on n-side of the junction and negative space charge region on p-side of the junction, an electric field directed from positive charge towards negative charge develops. Due to this field, an electron on p-side of the junction moves to n-side and a hole on nside of the junction moves to p-side. The motion of charge carriers due to the electric field is called drift. Thus a drift current, which is opposite in direction to the diffusion current (Fig.) starts.
Initially, diffusion current is large and drift current is small. As the diffusion process continues, the space-charge regions on either side of the junction extend, thus increasing the electric field strength and hence drift current. This process continues until the diffusion current equals the drift current. Thus a p-n junction is formed.
When p-type and n-type semiconductors are joined together, because of diffusion of charge carriers through the junction, a barrier is formed which opposes the further diffusion of charges through the junction. This barrier is called potential barrier. Thus, potential barrier in a PN junction refers to the potential required to overcome the barrier at the PN junction.

question_html:
What happens to the width of depletion layer of a p-n junction when it is

  1. forward biased?
  2. reverse biased?
question_text: What happens to the width of depletion layer of a p-n junction when it is forward biased? reverse biased?
explanation: During forward bias, carriers come close to junction and width of depletion layer decreases. During reverse bias, the carriers get away from the junction and width of depletion layer increases.
solution: During forward bias, carriers come close to junction and width of depletion layer decreases. During reverse bias, the carriers get away from the junction and width of depletion layer increases.
solution_html:
1. During forward bias, carriers come close to junction and width of depletion layer decreases.
  2. During reverse bias, the carriers get away from the junction and width of depletion layer increases.

question_html: How is forward biasing different from reverse biasing in a p-n junction diode?
question_text: How is forward biasing different from reverse biasing in a p-n junction diode?
explanation: Forward Bias: When an external d.c. source is connected to the junction diode with p-section connected to positive pole and n-section to the negative pole, the junction diode is said to be forward biased. Reverse Bias: When a battery is connected to junction diode with p-section connected to negative pole and n-section connected to the positive pole, the junction diode is said to be reverse biased.
solution: Forward Bias: When an external d.c. source is connected to the junction diode with p-section connected to positive pole and n-section to the negative pole, the junction diode is said to be forward biased. Reverse Bias: When a battery is connected to junction diode with p-section connected to negative pole and n-section connected to the positive pole, the junction diode is said to be reverse biased.
solution_html:
Forward Bias: When an external d.c. source is connected to the junction diode with p-section connected to positive pole and n-section to the negative pole, the junction diode is said to be forward biased.
Reverse Bias: When a battery is connected to junction diode with p-section connected to negative pole and n-section connected to the positive pole, the junction diode is said to be reverse biased.

explanation: In an n-type semiconductor, the pentavalent impurity atom shares four of its valence electrons with four tetravalent host atoms while its fifth electron remains free. This impurity atom as a whole is electrically neutral. So the semiconductor is also neutral.

question_html: Why can't we take one slab of p-type semiconductor and physically join it to another slab of n-type semiconductor to get a p-n junction?
question_text: Why can't we take one slab of p-type semiconductor and physically join it to another slab of n-type semiconductor to get a p-n junction?
explanation: A p-n junction cannot be obtained by physically joining the slabs of p-type and n-type semiconductors. The reason is that the surfaces of the two slabs may appear flat to the naked eye, but infact the size of irregularities on their surfaces is very large as compared to the interatomic spacing. When two such slabs are put together, a continuous contact at atomic level is not achieved. Due to this, there will be discontinuity at the junction of the two slabs and as a result, the charge carriers cannot flow from one slab to the other.
solution: A p-n junction cannot be obtained by physically joining the slabs of p-type and n-type semiconductors. The reason is that the surfaces of the two slabs may appear flat to the naked eye, but infact the size of irregularities on their surfaces is very large as compared to the interatomic spacing. When two such slabs are put together, a continuous contact at atomic level is not achieved. Due to this, there will be discontinuity at the junction of the two slabs and as a result, the charge carriers cannot flow from one slab to the other.

The reason is that the surfaces of the two slabs may appear flat to the naked eye, but infact the size of irregularities on their surfaces is very large as compared to the interatomic spacing. When two such slabs are put together, a continuous contact at atomic level is not achieved. Due to this, there will be discontinuity at the junction of the two slabs and as a result, the charge carriers cannot flow from one slab to the other.

question_html: On the basis of energy bands, distinguish between conductors, insulators and semiconductors.
question_text: On the basis of energy bands, distinguish between conductors, insulators and semiconductors.
explanation: Conductors. The energy band structure in solids have two possibilities : The valence band may be completely filled and the conduction band partially filled with an extremely small energy gap between them [Figure]. For example, in sodium, the conduction band is partially filled, while the valence band is completely filled. The valence band is completely filled and the conduction band is empty but the two overlap each other [Figure]. Zinc is an example of band overlap metals. Insulators. In insulators, the forbidden energy gap is quite large [Figure]. For example, the forbidden energy gap for diamond is 6 eV, which means that a minimum of 6 eV energy is required to make the electron jump from the completely filled valence band to the conduction band. When electric field is applied across such a solid, the electrons find it difficult to acquire such a large amount of energy and so the conduction band continues to be almost empty. No electron flow occurs i.e. no current flows through such solids. So they behave as insulators. Semiconductor. The energy band structure of the semiconductors is similar to the insulators but in their case; the size of the forbidden energy gap is much smaller than that for the insulators [Figure]. For example, the forbidden band for silicon is 1.1 eV. The electronic structure of the silicon (Si) is similar to that of the diamond, but due to the smaller width of the forbidden energy gap, the electrons in the valence band find it comparatively easier to shift to the conduction band. So, the conductivity of the silicon is inbetween the conductors and the insulators and it is termed as a semiconductor.
solution: Conductors. The energy band structure in solids have two possibilities : The valence band may be completely filled and the conduction band partially filled with an extremely small energy gap between them [Figure]. For example, in sodium, the conduction band is partially filled, while the valence band is completely filled. The valence band is completely filled and the conduction band is empty but the two overlap each other [Figure]. Zinc is an example of band overlap metals. Insulators. In insulators, the forbidden energy gap is quite large [Figure]. For example, the forbidden energy gap for diamond is 6 eV, which means that a minimum of 6 eV energy is required to make the electron jump from the completely filled valence band to the conduction band. When electric field is applied across such a solid, the electrons find it difficult to acquire such a large amount of energy and so the conduction band continues to be almost empty. No electron flow occurs i.e. no current flows through such solids. So they behave as insulators. Semiconductor. The energy band structure of the semiconductors is similar to the insulators but in their case; the size of the forbidden energy gap is much smaller than that for the insulators [Figure]. For example, the forbidden band for silicon is 1.1 eV. The electronic structure of the silicon (Si) is similar to that of the diamond, but due to the smaller width of the forbidden energy gap, the electrons in the valence band find it comparatively easier to shift to the conduction band. So, the conductivity of the silicon is inbetween the conductors and the insulators and it is termed as a semiconductor.
solution_html:
Conductors. The energy band structure in solids have two possibilities :

  1. The valence band may be completely filled and the conduction band partially filled with an extremely small energy gap between them [Figure]. For example, in sodium, the conduction band is partially filled, while the valence band is completely filled.
  2. The valence band is completely filled and the conduction band is empty but the two overlap each other [Figure]. Zinc is an example of band overlap metals.

Insulators. In insulators, the forbidden energy gap is quite large [Figure]. For example, the forbidden energy gap for diamond is 6 eV, which means that a minimum of 6 eV energy is required to make the electron jump from the completely filled valence band to the conduction band. When electric field is applied across such a solid, the electrons find it difficult to acquire such a large amount of energy and so the conduction band continues to be almost empty. No electron flow occurs i.e. no current flows through such solids. So they behave as insulators.

Semiconductor. The energy band structure of the semiconductors is similar to the insulators but in their case; the size of the forbidden energy gap is much smaller than that for the insulators [Figure]. For example, the forbidden band for silicon is 1.1 eV. The electronic structure of the silicon (Si) is similar to that of the diamond, but due to the smaller width of the forbidden energy gap, the electrons in the valence band find it comparatively easier to shift to the conduction band. So, the conductivity of the silicon is inbetween the conductors and the insulators and it is termed as a semiconductor.

subject: Physics
question_html: Why does a semiconductor get damaged when a heavy current flows through it?
question_text: Why does a semiconductor get damaged when a heavy current flows through it?
explanation: When a strong current is passed through a semiconductor, it heats up the semiconductor. Due to it, the large number of covalent bonds break up in semiconductor, resulting large number of charge carriers. As a result of it, the material starts behaving as a conductor. At this stage, the semiconductor losses the property of low conduction, hence it is said to be damaged.
solution: When a strong current is passed through a semiconductor, it heats up the semiconductor. Due to it, the large number of covalent bonds break up in semiconductor, resulting large number of charge carriers. As a result of it, the material starts behaving as a conductor. At this stage, the semiconductor losses the property of low conduction, hence it is said to be damaged.
solution_html: When a strong current is passed through a semiconductor, it heats up the semiconductor. Due to it, the large number of covalent bonds break up in semiconductor, resulting large number of charge carriers. As a result of it, the material starts behaving as a conductor. At this stage, the semiconductor losses the property of low conduction, hence it is said to be damaged.

1. Explain the formation of the depletion layer and potential barrier in a p-n junction.
  2. In Figure, the input waveform is converted into the output waveform by a device X. Name the device and draw its circuit diagram.
  3. Distinguish between a conductor, a semiconductor, and an insulator on the basis of energy band diagrams.
question_text: Explain the formation of the depletion layer and potential barrier in a p-n junction. In Figure, the input waveform is converted into the output waveform by a device X. Name the device and draw its circuit diagram. Distinguish between a conductor, a semiconductor, and an insulator on the basis of energy band diagrams.
explanation: A p-n junction is a basic semiconductor device. In the p-section, holes are the majority carriers; while in the n-section, the majority carriers are electrons. Due to the high concentration of different types of charge carriers in the two sections, holes from the p-region diffuse into the n-region, and electrons from the n-region diffuse into the p-region. In both cases, when an electron meets a hole, the two cancel the effect of each other and as a result, a thin layer at the junction becomes devoid of charge carriers. This is called the depletion layer as shown in Fig. Due to the diffusion of holes and electrons, the two sections of the junction diode no longer remain neutral. The p-section of the junction diode becomes slightly negative, while the n-section is rendered positive. It appears as if some fictitious battery is connected across the junction with its negative pole connected to the p-region and positive pole connected to the n-region. The potential difference developed across the junction due to migration of majority charge carriers is called a potential barrier . It opposes the further diffusion of charge carriers. The device X is a full-wave rectifier. Distinguishing features on the basis of energy band diagrams: Conductors Semiconductors Insulators 1 Either the conduction band is partially filled or the valence and conduction bands partly overlap. E g = 0 Valence and conduction bands are separated by a small energy gap. E g = 0.2 eV to 3 eV Valence and conduction bands are separated by a large energy gap. E g > 3eV 2 They have a large number of free electrons in the conduction band available for conduction. They have a very small number of free electrons in the conduction band available for conduction They have no free electrons in the conduction band and so do not conduct electricity.
solution: A p-n junction is a basic semiconductor device. In the p-section, holes are the majority carriers; while in the n-section, the majority carriers are electrons. Due to the high concentration of different types of charge carriers in the two sections, holes from the p-region diffuse into the n-region, and electrons from the n-region diffuse into the p-region. In both cases, when an electron meets a hole, the two cancel the effect of each other and as a result, a thin layer at the junction becomes devoid of charge carriers. This is called the depletion layer as shown in Fig. Due to the diffusion of holes and electrons, the two sections of the junction diode no longer remain neutral. The p-section of the junction diode becomes slightly negative, while the n-section is rendered positive. It appears as if some fictitious battery is connected across the junction with its negative pole connected to the p-region and positive pole connected to the n-region. The potential difference developed across the junction due to migration of majority charge carriers is called a potential barrier . It opposes the further diffusion of charge carriers. The device X is a full-wave rectifier. Distinguishing features on the basis of energy band diagrams: Conductors Semiconductors Insulators 1 Either the conduction band is partially filled or the valence and conduction bands partly overlap. E g = 0 Valence and conduction bands are separated by a small energy gap. E g = 0.2 eV to 3 eV Valence and conduction bands are separated by a large energy gap. E g > 3eV 2 They have a large number of free electrons in the conduction band available for conduction. They have a very small number of free electrons in the conduction band available for conduction They have no free electrons in the conduction band and so do not conduct electricity.
solution_html:
1. A p-n junction is a basic semiconductor device.
    In the p-section, holes are the majority carriers; while in the n-section, the majority carriers are electrons. Due to the high concentration of different types of charge carriers in the two sections, holes from the p-region diffuse into the n-region, and electrons from the n-region diffuse into the p-region. In both cases, when an electron meets a hole, the two cancel the effect of each other and as a result, a thin layer at the junction becomes devoid of charge carriers. This is called the depletion layer as shown in Fig.

    Due to the diffusion of holes and electrons, the two sections of the junction diode no longer remain neutral. The p-section of the junction diode becomes slightly negative, while the n-section is rendered positive. It appears as if some fictitious battery is connected across the junction with its negative pole connected to the p-region and positive pole connected to the n-region. The potential difference developed across the junction due to migration of majority charge carriers is called a potential barrier. It opposes the further diffusion of charge carriers.
  2. The device X is a full-wave rectifier.
  3. Distinguishing features on the basis of energy band diagrams:
     
      Conductors Semiconductors Insulators
    1 Either the conduction band is partially filled or the valence and conduction bands partly overlap.
    Eg = 0    		 Valence and conduction bands are separated by a small energy gap.
    Eg = 0.2 eV to 3 eV    		 Valence and conduction bands are separated by a large energy gap.
    Eg > 3eV
    2 They have a large number of free electrons in the conduction band available for conduction. They have a very small number of free electrons in the conduction band available for conduction They have no free electrons in the conduction band and so do not conduct electricity.

question_html:
1. Draw the circuit arrangement needed for studying the input and output characteristics of an n-p-n transistor in common emitter configuration. Draw the typical shape of these input and output characteristics. Why is it usually enough to determine only one input characteristics?
  2. The small signal current gain ({tex}\beta_{a c}{/tex}) of a transistor, can be taken as nearly equal to its dc current amplification factor ({tex}\beta_{d c}{/tex}). Why?
question_text: Draw the circuit arrangement needed for studying the input and output characteristics of an n-p-n transistor in common emitter configuration. Draw the typical shape of these input and output characteristics. Why is it usually enough to determine only one input characteristics? The small signal current gain ( {tex}\beta_{a c}{/tex} ) of a transistor, can be taken as nearly equal to its dc current amplification factor ( {tex}\beta_{d c}{/tex} ). Why?
explanation: Typical input and output characteristic The curve describes the changes in the values of input current with respect to the values of input voltage, keeping the output voltage constant. Therefore, it usually enough to determine only one input characteristics. Since I C increases with I B almost linearly and I C = 0 when I B = 0, the values of both {tex}\beta{/tex} dc and {tex}\beta{/tex} ac are nearly equal. So, for most calculations βdc can be used. Both βac and βdc vary with V CE and I B (or I C ) slightly.
solution: Typical input and output characteristic The curve describes the changes in the values of input current with respect to the values of input voltage, keeping the output voltage constant. Therefore, it usually enough to determine only one input characteristics. Since I C increases with I B almost linearly and I C = 0 when I B = 0, the values of both {tex}\beta{/tex} dc and {tex}\beta{/tex} ac are nearly equal. So, for most calculations βdc can be used. Both βac and βdc vary with V CE and I B (or I C ) slightly.
solution_html:
1. Typical input and output characteristic

    The curve describes the changes in the values of input current with respect to the values of input voltage, keeping the output voltage constant. Therefore, it usually enough to determine only one input characteristics.
  2. Since IC increases with IB almost linearly and IC = 0 when IB = 0, the values of both {tex}\beta{/tex}dc and {tex}\beta{/tex}ac are nearly equal. So, for most calculations βdc can be used. Both βac and βdc vary with VCE and IB (or IC) slightly.

question_html: What is a Zener diode? Draw its V-I characteristic. Explain with the help of a circuit diagram how a Zener diode can be used as a voltage regulator.
question_text: What is a Zener diode? Draw its V-I characteristic. Explain with the help of a circuit diagram how a Zener diode can be used as a voltage regulator.
explanation: Zener diode: A junction diode specially designed to operate only in the reverse breakdown region continuously (without getting damaged) is called a Zener diode. I-V characteristics Zener diode as a Voltage Regulator The Zener diode makes its use as a voltage regulator due to the following property: When a Zener diode is operated in the breakdown region, the voltage across it remains practically constant for a large change in the current. A simple circuit of a voltage regulator using a Zener diode is shown in the figure. The Zener diode is connected across load such that it is reverse biased. The series resistance R absorbs the output voltage fluctuations so as to maintain a constant voltage across the load. If the input dc voltage increases, the current through the R and Zener diode also increases. So, the voltage drop across R increases, without any change in the voltage across the Zener diode.

solution_html:
Zener diode: A junction diode specially designed to operate only in the reverse breakdown region continuously (without getting damaged) is called a Zener diode.
I-V characteristics

Zener diode as a Voltage Regulator
The Zener diode makes its use as a voltage regulator due to the following property:
When a Zener diode is operated in the breakdown region, the voltage across it remains practically constant for a large change in the current.
A simple circuit of a voltage regulator using a Zener diode is shown in the figure. The Zener diode is connected across load such that it is reverse biased.

The series resistance R absorbs the output voltage fluctuations so as to maintain a constant voltage across the load. If the input dc voltage increases, the current through the R and Zener diode also increases. So, the voltage drop across R increases, without any change in the voltage across the Zener diode.

question_html:
An AC signal is fed into two circuits 'X' and 'Y' and the corresponding output in the two cases have the wave forms as shown.

  1. Identify the circuits 'X' and 'Y'. Draw their labelled circuit diagrams.
  2. Briefly explain the working of circuit Y.
  3. How does the output waveform from circuit Y get modified when a capacitor is connected across the output terminals parallel to the load resistor?
question_text: An AC signal is fed into two circuits 'X' and 'Y' and the corresponding output in the two cases have the wave forms as shown. Identify the circuits 'X' and 'Y'. Draw their labelled circuit diagrams. Briefly explain the working of circuit Y. How does the output waveform from circuit Y get modified when a capacitor is connected across the output terminals parallel to the load resistor?
explanation: Here X is half-wave rectifier and Y is full-wave rectifier In case of a full wave rectifier during positive half cycle of ac, diode D 1 is forward biased and diode D 2 is reversed biased and output is taken across the load resistance with a particular polarity. Whereas during negative half cycle of ac, the diode D 2 is forward biased and diode D 1 is reverse biased, and again the output is taken across the load resistance with the same polarity. A capacitor of large capacitance is connected in parallel to the load resistor R L . When the pulsating voltage supplied by the rectifier is rising, the capacitor C gets charged. If there is no external load, the capacitor would have remained charged to the peak voltage of the rectified output. However, when there is no load and the rectified voltage starts falling, the capacitor gets discharged through the load and the voltage across capacitor begins to fall slowly.

A capacitor of large capacitance is connected in parallel to the load resistor R L . When the pulsating voltage supplied by the rectifier is rising, the capacitor C gets charged. If there is no external load, the capacitor would have remained charged to the peak voltage of the rectified output. However, when there is no load and the rectified voltage starts falling, the capacitor gets discharged through the load and the voltage across capacitor begins to fall slowly.

  2. Whereas during negative half cycle of ac, the diode D2 is forward biased and diode D1 is reverse biased, and again the output is taken across the load resistance with the same polarity.

    However, when there is no load and the rectified voltage starts falling, the capacitor gets discharged through the load and the voltage across capacitor begins to fall slowly.

question_html: At what temperature would an intrinsic semiconductor behave like a perfect insulator?
question_text: At what temperature would an intrinsic semiconductor behave like a perfect insulator?
explanation: At absolute zero temperature (0 K), the electrons in the valence band of semiconductors do not possess enough thermal energy to overcome the forbidden energy gap. So semiconductors do not conduct and behave as insulators.
solution: At absolute zero temperature (0 K), the electrons in the valence band of semiconductors do not possess enough thermal energy to overcome the forbidden energy gap. So semiconductors do not conduct and behave as insulators.

The following table provides the set of values, of V and I, obtained for a given diode:

Forward biasing Reverse biasing
V I V I
2 V 60 mA 0V 0 {tex}\mu{/tex} A
2.4 V 80 mA - 2 V - 0.25 {tex}\mu{/tex}A

Assuming the characteristics to be nearly linear, over this range, calculate the forward and reverse bias resistance of the given diode.
question_text: The following table provides the set of values, of V and I, obtained for a given diode: Forward biasing Reverse biasing V I V I 2 V 60 mA 0V 0 {tex}\mu{/tex} A 2.4 V 80 mA - 2 V - 0.25 {tex}\mu{/tex} A Assuming the characteristics to be nearly linear, over this range, calculate the forward and reverse bias resistance of the given diode.
explanation: During forward bias: {tex}\Delta{/tex} V = 2.4 - 2 = 0.4V and {tex}\Delta I{/tex} = 80 - 60 = 20 mA = 0.02 A {tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}r=\frac{\Delta V}{\Delta I}=\frac{0 \cdot 4}{0 \cdot 02}=20 \Omega{/tex} During reverse bias: {tex}\Delta{/tex} V = 0 - (-2) = 2V and {tex}\Delta I{/tex} = 0 - (-0.25) = 0.25 {tex}\mu{/tex} A = 0.25 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -6 A {tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}r=\frac{\Delta \mathrm{V}}{\Delta \mathrm{I}}=\frac{2}{0.25 \times 10^{-6}}=8 \times 10^{6} \Omega{/tex}
solution: During forward bias: {tex}\Delta{/tex} V = 2.4 - 2 = 0.4V and {tex}\Delta I{/tex} = 80 - 60 = 20 mA = 0.02 A {tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}r=\frac{\Delta V}{\Delta I}=\frac{0 \cdot 4}{0 \cdot 02}=20 \Omega{/tex} During reverse bias: {tex}\Delta{/tex} V = 0 - (-2) = 2V and {tex}\Delta I{/tex} = 0 - (-0.25) = 0.25 {tex}\mu{/tex} A = 0.25 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -6 A {tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}r=\frac{\Delta \mathrm{V}}{\Delta \mathrm{I}}=\frac{2}{0.25 \times 10^{-6}}=8 \times 10^{6} \Omega{/tex}

During forward bias: {tex}\Delta{/tex}V = 2.4 - 2 = 0.4V and {tex}\Delta I{/tex} = 80 - 60 = 20 mA = 0.02 A
{tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}r=\frac{\Delta V}{\Delta I}=\frac{0 \cdot 4}{0 \cdot 02}=20 \Omega{/tex} 
During reverse bias: {tex}\Delta{/tex}V = 0 - (-2) = 2V and {tex}\Delta I{/tex} = 0 - (-0.25) = 0.25 {tex}\mu{/tex}A = 0.25 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-6 A
{tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}r=\frac{\Delta \mathrm{V}}{\Delta \mathrm{I}}=\frac{2}{0.25 \times 10^{-6}}=8 \times 10^{6} \Omega{/tex}

question_html:
Explain the formation of energy bands in solids. On the basis of energy band diagrams, distinguish between

  1. a metal,
  2. an insulator and
  3. a semiconductor.
question_text: Explain the formation of energy bands in solids. On the basis of energy band diagrams, distinguish between a metal, an insulator and a semiconductor.
explanation: Formation of energy bands in solid: In a single isolated atom, the electrons in each orbit have definite energy associated with it. But in the case of solids all the atoms are close to each other, so the energy levels of outermost orbit electrons are affected by the neighbouring atoms. When two single or isolated atoms are brought close to each other than the outermost orbit. The electrons in the outermost orbit of one atom experience an attractive force from the nearest or neighbouring atomic nucleus. Due to this, the energies of the electrons will not be at the same level, the energy levels of electrons are changed to a value which is higher or lower than that of the original energy level of the electron. Insulator: Insulator is material, in which there is a large energy gap between the conduction band and the valence band, A large amount of energy is required to shift the electron from valence band to conduction band. Semiconductor: Semiconductor is the material, in which there is a less energy gap required with respect to the insulator, When some amount of energy flow in the semiconductor, then it allows to flow the electron from valence band to conduction band. Conductor: Conductor is the material in which, there is no energy gap between the conduction band and valence band, so no extra energy is needed to flow the electron from valence band to conduction band.
solution: Formation of energy bands in solid: In a single isolated atom, the electrons in each orbit have definite energy associated with it. But in the case of solids all the atoms are close to each other, so the energy levels of outermost orbit electrons are affected by the neighbouring atoms. The electrons in the outermost orbit of one atom experience an attractive force from the nearest or neighbouring atomic nucleus. Conductor: Conductor is the material in which, there is no energy gap between the conduction band and valence band, so no extra energy is needed to flow the electron from valence band to conduction band.

But in the case of solids all the atoms are close to each other, so the energy levels of outermost orbit electrons are affected by the neighbouring atoms. When two single or isolated atoms are brought close to each other than the outermost orbit. The electrons in the outermost orbit of one atom experience an attractive force from the nearest or neighbouring atomic nucleus. Due to this, the energies of the electrons will not be at the same level, the energy levels of electrons are changed to a value which is higher or lower than that of the original energy level of the electron.

  1. Insulator: Insulator is material, in which there is a large energy gap between the conduction band and the valence band, A large amount of energy is required to shift the electron from valence band to conduction band.
  2. Semiconductor: Semiconductor is the material, in which there is a less energy gap required with respect to the insulator, When some amount of energy flow in the semiconductor, then it allows to flow the electron from valence band to conduction band.
  3. Conductor: Conductor is the material in which, there is no energy gap between the conduction band and valence band, so no extra energy is needed to flow the electron from valence band to conduction band.

subject: Physics
question_html: What is an intrinsic semiconductor? How can this material be converted into (i) p-type, (ii) n-type extrinsic semiconductor? Explain with the help of energy band diagrams.
question_text: What is an intrinsic semiconductor? How can this material be converted into (i) p-type, (ii) n-type extrinsic semiconductor? Explain with the help of energy band diagrams.
explanation: Semiconductors in which the number of electrons n e is equal to the number of holes n h are intrinsic semiconductors. When a small amount of suitable impurity is added to the intrinsic semiconductor we can convert it into an extrinsic semiconductor of either p-type or n-type. Doping changes the concentration of charge carriers in the element. p-type: When Si or Ge is doped with any trivalent impurity like AI, B, etc we get a p-type semiconductor. The dopant has one valence electron less. Thus, this atom form a covalent bond with the neighboring three atoms and is less of one electron to offer to the fourth silicon atom and as a result, there is a vacancy. Hence, an electron in the outer orbit of the neighboring atom may jump to fill this vacancy leaving a vacancy or hole at its own site. This hole is the conducting charge carrier. Therefore, doping with a trivalent impurity gives us a p-type semiconductor. The energy band diagram for a p-type semiconductor is as shown below: n-type: When we dope Si or Ge with pentavalent impurities like As, P then four electrons of this atom will form covalent bonds with the neighboring Si atom in the lattice. Whereas, the fifth electron will remain loosely bound to its parent atom. Hence, the ionization energy which is required to make this electron free is very less and it will move around even at room temperature. Thus, the pentavalent dopant will donate one extra electron for conduction and this acts as an n-type semiconductor. The energy band diagram for n-type semiconductor is as shown below:

The energy band diagram for a p-type semiconductor is as shown below: n-type: When we dope Si or Ge with pentavalent impurities like As, P then four electrons of this atom will form covalent bonds with the neighboring Si atom in the lattice. Whereas, the fifth electron will remain loosely bound to its parent atom. Hence, the ionization energy which is required to make this electron free is very less and it will move around even at room temperature. Thus, the pentavalent dopant will donate one extra electron for conduction and this acts as an n-type semiconductor. The energy band diagram for n-type semiconductor is as shown below:

Semiconductors in which the number of electrons ne is equal to the number of holes nh are intrinsic semiconductors.
When a small amount of suitable impurity is added to the intrinsic semiconductor we can convert it into an extrinsic semiconductor of either p-type or n-type. Doping changes the concentration of charge carriers in the element.

  1. p-type: When Si or Ge is doped with any trivalent impurity like AI, B, etc we get a p-type semiconductor. The dopant has one valence electron less. Thus, this atom form a covalent bond with the neighboring three atoms and is less of one electron to offer to the fourth silicon atom and as a result, there is a vacancy. Hence, an electron in the outer orbit of the neighboring atom may jump to fill this vacancy leaving a vacancy or hole at its own site. This hole is the conducting charge carrier. Therefore, doping with a trivalent impurity gives us a p-type semiconductor.
    The energy band diagram for a p-type semiconductor is as shown below:
  2. n-type: When we dope Si or Ge with pentavalent impurities like As, P then four electrons of this atom will form covalent bonds with the neighboring Si atom in the lattice. Whereas, the fifth electron will remain loosely bound to its parent atom. Hence, the ionization energy which is required to make this electron free is very less and it will move around even at room temperature. Thus, the pentavalent dopant will donate one extra electron for conduction and this acts as an n-type semiconductor.

question_html: Explain (i) forward biasing, (ii) reverse biasing of the p-n junction diode. With the help of a circuit diagram, explain the use of this device as a half-wave rectifier.
question_text: Explain (i) forward biasing, (ii) reverse biasing of the p-n junction diode. With the help of a circuit diagram, explain the use of this device as a half-wave rectifier.
explanation: When positive terminal of the battery is connected to p-region and the negative terminal of the battery is connected to n-region of the junction diode, it is said to be forward biasing. When the positive terminal of the battery is connected to n-region & the negative terminal is connected to p-region of the p-n junction it is said to be reverse biasing. Working: During one half of the input a.c., the diode is forward biased and a current flows through R L . During the other half of the input a.c., the diode is reverse biased and no current flows through the load R L . Hence, the given a.v. input is rectified.
solution: When positive terminal of the battery is connected to p-region and the negative terminal of the battery is connected to n-region of the junction diode, it is said to be forward biasing. When the positive terminal of the battery is connected to n-region & the negative terminal is connected to p-region of the p-n junction it is said to be reverse biasing. Working: During one half of the input a.c., the diode is forward biased and a current flows through R L . During the other half of the input a.c., the diode is reverse biased and no current flows through the load R L . Hence, the given a.v. input is rectified.
solution_html:
When positive terminal of the battery is connected to p-region and the negative terminal of the battery is connected to n-region of the junction diode, it is said to be forward biasing.
When the positive terminal of the battery is connected to n-region & the negative terminal is connected to p-region of the p-n junction it is said to be reverse biasing.

Working: During one half of the input a.c., the diode is forward biased and a current flows through RL.
During the other half of the input a.c., the diode is reverse biased and no current flows through the load RL. Hence, the given a.v. input is rectified.

question_text: A semiconductor has equal electron and hole concentration of 6 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 8 m -3 . On doping with certain impurity, electron concentration increases to 9 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 12 m -3 . Identify the new semiconductor obtained after doping. Calculate the new hole concentration. How does the energy gap vary with doping?
explanation: As the electron concentration increases after doping, so the new semiconductor obtained is of n-type. As n e n h = {tex}n_{i}^{2}{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} n h = {tex}\frac{n_{i}^{2}}{n_{e}}=\frac{\left(6 \times 10^{8}\right)^{2}}{9 \times 10^{12}}{/tex} = 4 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 4 m -3 The energy gap decreases with doping.
solution: As the electron concentration increases after doping, so the new semiconductor obtained is of n-type. As n e n h = {tex}n_{i}^{2}{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} n h = {tex}\frac{n_{i}^{2}}{n_{e}}=\frac{\left(6 \times 10^{8}\right)^{2}}{9 \times 10^{12}}{/tex} = 4 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 4 m -3 The energy gap decreases with doping.
solution_html:
1. As the electron concentration increases after doping, so the new semiconductor obtained is of n-type.
  2. As ne nh = {tex}n_{i}^{2}{/tex}
    {tex}\therefore{/tex} nh = {tex}\frac{n_{i}^{2}}{n_{e}}=\frac{\left(6 \times 10^{8}\right)^{2}}{9 \times 10^{12}}{/tex} = 4 {tex}\times{/tex} 104 m-3
  3. The energy gap decreases with doping.

question_text: The V-I characteristic of a silicon diode is given in the figure. Calculate the diode resistance in: forward bias at V = {tex}\pm{/tex} 2 V and V = + 1 V, and reverse bias V = - 1 V and - 2 V
explanation: The forward bias diode resistance is given by {tex}r_{\mathrm{fb}}=\frac{\Delta V}{\Delta I}{/tex} where {tex}\Delta V{/tex} and {tex}\Delta I{/tex} are the small changes in the voltage and current near the desired values. r fb (at + 2V) = {tex}\frac{(2.2-1.8) \mathrm{V}}{(80-60) \mathrm{mA}}=\frac{0.4 \mathrm{~V}}{20 \times 10^{-3} \mathrm{~A}}{/tex} = 20 {tex}\Omega{/tex} r fb (at 1V) = {tex}\frac{(1.2-0.8) \mathrm{V}}{(20-10) \mathrm{mA}}=\frac{0.4 \mathrm{~V}}{10 \times 10^{-3} \mathrm{~A}}{/tex} = 40 {tex}\Omega{/tex} In the reverse bias characteristic, the non-linearity in the V - I curve is small. The slopes of the V-I curve at -1V and -2V are nearly equal r rb (-2V) = {tex}\frac{-2 \mathrm{~V}}{-0.25 \mu \mathrm{A}}=\frac{2 \mathrm{~V}}{0.25 \times 10^{-6} \mathrm{~A}}{/tex} = 8 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 6 {tex}\Omega{/tex} Also, r rb (-1 V) {tex}\approx{/tex} 8 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 6 {tex}\Omega{/tex}
solution: The forward bias diode resistance is given by {tex}r_{\mathrm{fb}}=\frac{\Delta V}{\Delta I}{/tex} where {tex}\Delta V{/tex} and {tex}\Delta I{/tex} are the small changes in the voltage and current near the desired values. r fb (at + 2V) = {tex}\frac{(2.2-1.8) \mathrm{V}}{(80-60) \mathrm{mA}}=\frac{0.4 \mathrm{~V}}{20 \times 10^{-3} \mathrm{~A}}{/tex} = 20 {tex}\Omega{/tex} r fb (at 1V) = {tex}\frac{(1.2-0.8) \mathrm{V}}{(20-10) \mathrm{mA}}=\frac{0.4 \mathrm{~V}}{10 \times 10^{-3} \mathrm{~A}}{/tex} = 40 {tex}\Omega{/tex} In the reverse bias characteristic, the non-linearity in the V - I curve is small. The slopes of the V-I curve at -1V and -2V are nearly equal r rb (-2V) = {tex}\frac{-2 \mathrm{~V}}{-0.25 \mu \mathrm{A}}=\frac{2 \mathrm{~V}}{0.25 \times 10^{-6} \mathrm{~A}}{/tex} = 8 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 6 {tex}\Omega{/tex} Also, r rb (-1 V) {tex}\approx{/tex} 8 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 6 {tex}\Omega{/tex}
solution_html:
1. The forward bias diode resistance is given by
    {tex}r_{\mathrm{fb}}=\frac{\Delta V}{\Delta I}{/tex}
    where {tex}\Delta V{/tex} and {tex}\Delta I{/tex} are the small changes in the voltage and current near the desired values.
    rfb (at + 2V) = {tex}\frac{(2.2-1.8) \mathrm{V}}{(80-60) \mathrm{mA}}=\frac{0.4 \mathrm{~V}}{20 \times 10^{-3} \mathrm{~A}}{/tex} = 20 {tex}\Omega{/tex}
    rfb (at 1V) = {tex}\frac{(1.2-0.8) \mathrm{V}}{(20-10) \mathrm{mA}}=\frac{0.4 \mathrm{~V}}{10 \times 10^{-3} \mathrm{~A}}{/tex} = 40 {tex}\Omega{/tex}
  2. In the reverse bias characteristic, the non-linearity in the V - I curve is small. The slopes of the V-I curve at -1V and -2V are nearly equal
    rrb (-2V) = {tex}\frac{-2 \mathrm{~V}}{-0.25 \mu \mathrm{A}}=\frac{2 \mathrm{~V}}{0.25 \times 10^{-6} \mathrm{~A}}{/tex} 
    = 8 {tex}\times{/tex} 106 {tex}\Omega{/tex}
    Also, rrb (-1 V) {tex}\approx{/tex} 8 {tex}\times{/tex} 106 {tex}\Omega{/tex}

question_text: Can two p-n junction diodes place back to back work as a p-n-p transistor?
explanation: No. In that case, the n-region forming the base will become quite thick and most of the majority charge carriers rushing from emitter to collector will get neutralised. The transistor will not work.

subject: Physics
question_html: Explain the advantages and disadvantages of semiconducting devices compared to vacuum tube devices.
question_text: Explain the advantages and disadvantages of semiconducting devices compared to vacuum tube devices.
explanation: Advantages of semiconductor diodes over vacuum diodes: No cathode heating is required in a junction diode for the production of charge carriers. A p-n junction diode is much smaller in size, robust and compact, and hence easy to handle. It starts operating as soon as it is switched on. It can be used for much higher frequencies. It dissipates less heat and is highly efficient in operation. It has a much longer life. The voltage drop across a junction diode is very small compared to that across a vacuum diode. No vacuum has to be created for its operation. Disadvantage: It is highly sensitive to temperature and gets burnt up even if a moderately high current is passed through it.
solution: Advantages of semiconductor diodes over vacuum diodes: No cathode heating is required in a junction diode for the production of charge carriers. A p-n junction diode is much smaller in size, robust and compact, and hence easy to handle. It starts operating as soon as it is switched on. It can be used for much higher frequencies. It dissipates less heat and is highly efficient in operation. It has a much longer life. The voltage drop across a junction diode is very small compared to that across a vacuum diode. No vacuum has to be created for its operation. Disadvantage: It is highly sensitive to temperature and gets burnt up even if a moderately high current is passed through it.
solution_html:
Advantages of semiconductor diodes over vacuum diodes:

  1. No cathode heating is required in a junction diode for the production of charge carriers.
  2. A p-n junction diode is much smaller in size, robust and compact, and hence easy to handle.
  3. It starts operating as soon as it is switched on.
  4. It can be used for much higher frequencies.
  5. It dissipates less heat and is highly efficient in operation.
  6. It has a much longer life.
  7. The voltage drop across a junction diode is very small compared to that across a vacuum diode.
  8. No vacuum has to be created for its operation.

Disadvantage: It is highly sensitive to temperature and gets burnt up even if a moderately high current is passed through it.

question_html: What are the limitations of intrinsic semiconductors when we use them for developing semiconductor devices?
question_text: What are the limitations of intrinsic semiconductors when we use them for developing semiconductor devices?
explanation: When intrinsic semiconductors are used for developing semiconductor devices, they have many limitations as discussed below: Intrinsic semiconductors have low intrinsic charge carrier concentration (of hole and electrons) {tex}\approx{/tex} 10 6 m -3 . So they have low electrical conductivity. As intrinsic charge carriers are always thermally generated, so flexibility is not available to control their number. For intrinsic semiconductors, n e = n h . They cannot have a predominant hole or electron conduction. This puts a limit on the usefulness of such materials.
solution: When intrinsic semiconductors are used for developing semiconductor devices, they have many limitations as discussed below: Intrinsic semiconductors have low intrinsic charge carrier concentration (of hole and electrons) {tex}\approx{/tex} 10 6 m -3 . So they have low electrical conductivity. As intrinsic charge carriers are always thermally generated, so flexibility is not available to control their number. For intrinsic semiconductors, n e = n h . They cannot have a predominant hole or electron conduction. This puts a limit on the usefulness of such materials.
solution_html:
When intrinsic semiconductors are used for developing semiconductor devices, they have many limitations as discussed below:

  1. Intrinsic semiconductors have low intrinsic charge carrier concentration (of hole and electrons) {tex}\approx{/tex} 106 m-3. So they have low electrical conductivity.
  2. As intrinsic charge carriers are always thermally generated, so flexibility is not available to control their number.
  3. For intrinsic semiconductors, ne = nh. They cannot have a predominant hole or electron conduction. This puts a limit on the usefulness of such materials.

explanation: The circuit shown in figure (a) can be redrawn as shown in figure. The battery of 10V makes p-section at +10 V, while the battery of 5 V makes n-section at +5 V. Similarly, by redrawing the circuit shown in Figure (b), it can be shown that the p-section is at net +7 V w.r.t. n-section. Hence the diode is forward biased .

question_html: Name the type of bias that results in very high resistance of a p-n junction diode. In the given circuit, a voltmeter V is connected across bulb B. What changes would occur in bulb B and voltmeter V, if the resistor R is increased in value? Give reason for your answer.
question_text: Name the type of bias that results in very high resistance of a p-n junction diode. In the given circuit, a voltmeter V is connected across bulb B . What changes would occur in bulb B and voltmeter V , if the resistor R is increased in value? Give reason for your answer.
explanation: The reverse biasing results in very high resistance across the p-n junction. If the value of the resistance R is increased, the current in the forward-biased input circuit decreases. The emitter current I E decreases and hence the collector current (I C = I E - I B ) also decreases. The glowness of the bulb decreases. Due to the decrease in I C , the potential drop across bulb B decreases, and hence the voltmeter shows a lower voltage.
solution: The reverse biasing results in very high resistance across the p-n junction. If the value of the resistance R is increased, the current in the forward-biased input circuit decreases. The emitter current I E decreases and hence the collector current (I C = I E - I B ) also decreases. The glowness of the bulb decreases. Due to the decrease in I C , the potential drop across bulb B decreases, and hence the voltmeter shows a lower voltage.
solution_html:
The reverse biasing results in very high resistance across the p-n junction.

If the value of the resistance R is increased, the current in the forward-biased input circuit decreases. The emitter current IE decreases and hence the collector current (IC = IE - IB) also decreases. The glowness of the bulb decreases. Due to the decrease in IC, the potential drop across bulb B decreases, and hence the voltmeter shows a lower voltage.

question_html: Draw the energy band diagram of a p-type semiconductor. Deduce an expression for the conductivity of a p-type semiconductor.
question_text: Draw the energy band diagram of a p-type semiconductor. Deduce an expression for the conductivity of a p-type semiconductor.
explanation: For energy band diagram, The expression for the conductivity of a p-type semiconductor: A p-type semiconductor has holes as the majority charge carriers. {tex}\therefore{/tex} I = I h = {tex}e n_{h} A v_{h}{/tex} Also R = {tex}\rho \frac{l}{A}{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} V = RI = {tex}\rho \frac{l}{A} \cdot e n_{h} A v_{h}{/tex} E = {tex}\frac{V}{l}=\rho e n_{h} v_{h}=\rho e n_{h} \cdot \mu_{h} E{/tex} [ {tex}\because{/tex} {tex}\mu_{h}=\frac{v_{h}}{E}{/tex} ] {tex}\therefore{/tex} Conductivity, {tex}\rho=\frac{1}{\rho}=e n_{h} \mu_{h}{/tex}
solution: For energy band diagram, The expression for the conductivity of a p-type semiconductor: A p-type semiconductor has holes as the majority charge carriers. {tex}\therefore{/tex} I = I h = {tex}e n_{h} A v_{h}{/tex} Also R = {tex}\rho \frac{l}{A}{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} V = RI = {tex}\rho \frac{l}{A} \cdot e n_{h} A v_{h}{/tex} E = {tex}\frac{V}{l}=\rho e n_{h} v_{h}=\rho e n_{h} \cdot \mu_{h} E{/tex} [ {tex}\because{/tex} {tex}\mu_{h}=\frac{v_{h}}{E}{/tex} ] {tex}\therefore{/tex} Conductivity, {tex}\rho=\frac{1}{\rho}=e n_{h} \mu_{h}{/tex}
solution_html:
For energy band diagram,

The expression for the conductivity of a p-type semiconductor: A p-type semiconductor has holes as the majority charge carriers.
{tex}\therefore{/tex} I = Ih = {tex}e n_{h} A v_{h}{/tex} 
Also R = {tex}\rho \frac{l}{A}{/tex} 
{tex}\therefore{/tex} V = RI = {tex}\rho \frac{l}{A} \cdot e n_{h} A v_{h}{/tex} 
E = {tex}\frac{V}{l}=\rho e n_{h} v_{h}=\rho e n_{h} \cdot \mu_{h} E{/tex}  [{tex}\because{/tex} {tex}\mu_{h}=\frac{v_{h}}{E}{/tex}]
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Conductivity, {tex}\rho=\frac{1}{\rho}=e n_{h} \mu_{h}{/tex}

question_html: Explain the variation of resistivity with temperature in pure-semiconductors.
question_text: Explain the variation of resistivity with temperature in pure-semiconductors.
explanation: The resistivity of a semiconductor is given by {tex}\rho=\frac{1}{\sigma}=\frac{1}{e\left(n_{e} \mu_{e}+n_{h} \mu_{h}\right)}{/tex} As the temperature increases, the mobilities {tex}\mu_e{/tex} and {tex}\mu_h{/tex} of electrons and holes decrease due to the increase in their collision frequency. But due to the small energy gap of semiconductors, more and more electrons {tex}\left(n \propto e^{-E_{g} / k_{B} T}\right){/tex} from the valence band cross over to the conduction band. The increase in carrier concentrations, n e and n h is so large that decrease in the values of {tex}\mu_e{/tex} and {tex}\mu_h{/tex} has no influence. The overall effect is that conductivity increases or resistivity decreases with the increase of temperature.
solution: The resistivity of a semiconductor is given by {tex}\rho=\frac{1}{\sigma}=\frac{1}{e\left(n_{e} \mu_{e}+n_{h} \mu_{h}\right)}{/tex} As the temperature increases, the mobilities {tex}\mu_e{/tex} and {tex}\mu_h{/tex} of electrons and holes decrease due to the increase in their collision frequency. But due to the small energy gap of semiconductors, more and more electrons {tex}\left(n \propto e^{-E_{g} / k_{B} T}\right){/tex} from the valence band cross over to the conduction band. The increase in carrier concentrations, n e and n h is so large that decrease in the values of {tex}\mu_e{/tex} and {tex}\mu_h{/tex} has no influence. The overall effect is that conductivity increases or resistivity decreases with the increase of temperature.
solution_html:
The resistivity of a semiconductor is given by
{tex}\rho=\frac{1}{\sigma}=\frac{1}{e\left(n_{e} \mu_{e}+n_{h} \mu_{h}\right)}{/tex}
As the temperature increases, the mobilities {tex}\mu_e{/tex} and {tex}\mu_h{/tex} of electrons and holes decrease due to the increase in their collision frequency. But due to the small energy gap of semiconductors, more and more electrons {tex}\left(n \propto e^{-E_{g} / k_{B} T}\right){/tex} from the valence band cross over to the conduction band. The increase in carrier concentrations, ne and nh is so large that decrease in the values of {tex}\mu_e{/tex} and {tex}\mu_h{/tex} has no influence. The overall effect is that conductivity increases or resistivity decreases with the increase of temperature.

subject: Physics
question_html: Why does the conductivity of a semiconductor increase with rise of temperature?
question_text: Why does the conductivity of a semiconductor increase with rise of temperature?
explanation: When a semiconductor is heated, more and more electrons jump across the forbidden gap from the valence band to the conduction band where these are free to conduct electricity. Hence the conductivity increases with the increase in temperature.
solution: When a semiconductor is heated, more and more electrons jump across the forbidden gap from the valence band to the conduction band where these are free to conduct electricity. Hence the conductivity increases with the increase in temperature.
solution_html: When a semiconductor is heated, more and more electrons jump across the forbidden gap from the valence band to the conduction band where these are free to conduct electricity. Hence the conductivity increases with the increase in temperature.

question_html: When the voltage drop across a p-n junction diode is increased from 0.70 V to 0.71 V, the change in the diode current is 10 mA. What is the dynamic resistance of the diode?
question_text: When the voltage drop across a p-n junction diode is increased from 0.70 V to 0.71 V, the change in the diode current is 10 mA. What is the dynamic resistance of the diode?
explanation: For a p-n junction diode, the dynamic resistance is given by: r = {tex}\frac{\Delta V}{\Delta I}{/tex} Given that: {tex}\Delta V{/tex} = 0.71 - 0.70 = 0.01 V = 10 -2 V {tex}\Delta I{/tex} = 10 -3 {tex}\times{/tex} 10A = 10 -2 A {tex}\therefore{/tex} r = {tex}\frac{10^{-2}}{10^{-2}}{/tex} = 1 {tex}\Omega{/tex}
solution: For a p-n junction diode, the dynamic resistance is given by: r = {tex}\frac{\Delta V}{\Delta I}{/tex} Given that: {tex}\Delta V{/tex} = 0.71 - 0.70 = 0.01 V = 10 -2 V {tex}\Delta I{/tex} = 10 -3 {tex}\times{/tex} 10A = 10 -2 A {tex}\therefore{/tex} r = {tex}\frac{10^{-2}}{10^{-2}}{/tex} = 1 {tex}\Omega{/tex}
solution_html:
For a p-n junction diode, the dynamic resistance is given by:
r = {tex}\frac{\Delta V}{\Delta I}{/tex}
Given that:
{tex}\Delta V{/tex} = 0.71 - 0.70 = 0.01 V = 10-2 V
{tex}\Delta I{/tex} = 10-3 {tex}\times{/tex} 10A = 10-2 A
{tex}\therefore{/tex} r = {tex}\frac{10^{-2}}{10^{-2}}{/tex} = 1{tex}\Omega{/tex}

question_text: A square wave (-1V to 1V) is applied to the p-n junction diode as shown below. Draw the output waveform.
explanation: The p-side of the diode is earthed, it is at zero potential. So the diode conducts current when the input level is - 1V and does not conduct when the input level is +1V. As the diode is ideal, the output across it will be either 0 or - 1V, as shown in the figure.

question_html: If N is Avogadro number, M is atomic weight and {tex}\rho{/tex}, the density, the number of free electrons per unit volume of a monovalent metallic crystal can be expressed as
question_text: If N is Avogadro number, M is atomic weight and {tex}\rho{/tex} , the density, the number of free electrons per unit volume of a monovalent metallic crystal can be expressed as

options.explanation: As the crystal is monovalent, it has one free electron per atom. If V is the volume of M mass of the crystal, it will contain N atoms or N free electrons. Therefore, the number of free electrons per unit volume, n = {tex}\frac{N}{V}=\frac{N}{M / \rho}=\frac{\rho N}{M}{/tex} [ {tex}\because{/tex} {tex}\rho= \frac{M}{V}{/tex} ]

subject: Physics
question_html: Why a transistor cannot be used as a rectifier?
question_text: Why a transistor cannot be used as a rectifier?
explanation: To use a transistor as a rectifier, either its emitter-base portion or collector base portion has to be used. As the base is thin and lightly doped, either of the two portions will not work as a p-n junction. So a transistor cannot be used as a rectifier.
